					<h1 id="firstHeading" class="firstHeading mw-first-heading"><span lang="en" dir="ltr"><span class="mw-page-title-main">Mars</span></span></h1>
							
<div id="p-lang-btn" class="vector-dropdown mw-portlet mw-portlet-lang"  >
	<input type="checkbox" id="p-lang-btn-checkbox" role="button" aria-haspopup="true" data-event-name="ui.dropdown-p-lang-btn" class="vector-dropdown-checkbox mw-interlanguage-selector" aria-label="Go to an article in another language. Available in 255 languages"   >

	</label>

</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Fourth planet from the Sun</div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}</style><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">This article is about the planet. For the deity, see <a href="/wiki/Mars_(mythology)" title="Mars (mythology)">Mars (mythology)</a>. For other uses, see <a href="/wiki/Mars_(disambiguation)" class="mw-disambig" title="Mars (disambiguation)">Mars (disambiguation)</a>.</div>

data-sort-value="7004887752439999999♠"></span>1.027<span style="margin-left:.25em;">491</span><span style="margin-left:.25em;">25</span>&#160;d</span><sup id="cite&#95;ref-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-14"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><br /> <span class="nowrap">24<sup>h</sup> 39<sup>m</sup> 36<sup>s</sup></span></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label" style="max-width:11em;"><div style="display: inline-block; line-height: 1.2em; padding: .1em 0;"><a </tbody></table></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label" style="max-width:11em;">Surface <a href="/wiki/Absorbed_dose" title="Absorbed dose">absorbed dose</a> <a href="/wiki/Dose_rate" title="Dose rate">rate</a></th><td class="infobox-data">8.8&#160;μGy/h<sup id="cite&#95;ref-surface-radiation&#95;20-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-surface-radiation-20"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label" style="max-width:11em;">Surface <a href="/wiki/Equivalent_dose" title="Equivalent dose">equivalent dose</a> <a href="/wiki/Dose_rate" title="Dose rate">rate</a></th><td class="infobox-data">27&#160;μSv/h<sup id="cite&#95;ref-surface-radiation&#95;20-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-surface-radiation-20"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label" style="max-width:11em;"><div style="display: inline-block; line-height: 1.2em; padding: .1em 0;"><a href="/wiki/Absolute_magnitude#Solar_System_bodies_(H)" title="Absolute magnitude">Absolute magnitude&#160;<i>(H)</i></a></div></th><td class="infobox-data">−1.5<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IMCCE&#95;22-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IMCCE-22"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label" style="max-width:11em;"><div style="display: inline-block; line-height: 1.2em; padding: .1em 0;"><a href="/wiki/Angular_diameter" title="Angular diameter">Angular diameter</a></div></th><td class="infobox-data">3.5–25.1 <a href="/wiki/Arcsecond" class="mw-redirect" title="Arcsecond">arcseconds</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NasaFactSheet&#95;3-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NasaFactSheet-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></td></tr><tr><th colspan="2" class="infobox-header" style="background-color:LightSalmon; color:inherit;">Atmosphere<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NasaFactSheet&#95;3-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NasaFactSheet-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-barlow08&#95;23-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-barlow08-23"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></th></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label" style="max-width:11em;"><div style="display: inline-block; line-height: 1.2em; padding: .1em 0;">Surface <a href="/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure" title="Atmospheric pressure">pressure</a></div></th><td class="infobox-data">0.636 (0.4–0.87)&#160;<a href="/wiki/Pascal_(unit)" title="Pascal (unit)">kPa</a><br />0.00628&#160;<a href="/wiki/Atmosphere_(unit)" class="mw-redirect" title="Atmosphere (unit)">atm</a></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label" style="max-width:11em;"><a href="/wiki/Atmospheric_chemistry#Atmospheric_composition" title="Atmospheric chemistry">Composition by volume</a></th><td class="infobox-data"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1126788409" /><div class="plainlist">
<ul><li>95.97% <a href="/wiki/Carbon_dioxide" title="Carbon dioxide">carbon dioxide</a></li>
<li>1.93% <a href="/wiki/Argon" title="Argon">argon</a></li>
<li>1.89% <a href="/wiki/Nitrogen" title="Nitrogen">nitrogen</a></li>
<li>0.146% <a href="/wiki/Oxygen" title="Oxygen">oxygen</a></li>
<li>0.0557% <a href="/wiki/Carbon_monoxide" title="Carbon monoxide">carbon monoxide</a></li>
<li>0.0210% <a href="/wiki/Water_vapor" title="Water vapor">water vapor</a></li></ul>
</div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" class="infobox-below"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1327269900">.mw-parser-output .reflist-columns-2{column-width:30em}.mw-parser-output .reflist-columns-3{column-width:25em}body.skin-vector-2022 .mw-parser-output .reflist-columns-2{column-width:27em}body.skin-vector-2022 .mw-parser-output .reflist-columns-3{column-width:22.5em}.mw-parser-output .references[data-mw-group=upper-alpha]{list-style-type:upper-alpha}.mw-parser-output .references[data-mw-group=upper-roman]{list-style-type:upper-roman}.mw-parser-output .references[data-mw-group=lower-alpha]{list-style-type:lower-alpha}.mw-parser-output .references[data-mw-group=lower-greek]{list-style-type:lower-greek}.mw-parser-output .references[data-mw-group=lower-roman]{list-style-type:lower-roman}.mw-parser-output div.reflist-liststyle-upper-alpha .references{list-style-type:upper-alpha}.mw-parser-output div.reflist-liststyle-upper-roman .references{list-style-type:upper-roman}.mw-parser-output div.reflist-liststyle-lower-alpha .references{list-style-type:lower-alpha}.mw-parser-output div.reflist-liststyle-lower-greek .references{list-style-type:lower-greek}.mw-parser-output div.reflist-liststyle-lower-roman .references{list-style-type:lower-roman}</style><div>
</div></td></tr></tbody></table>
<p><b>Mars</b> is the fourth <a href="/wiki/Planet" title="Planet">planet</a> from the <a href="/wiki/Sun" title="Sun">Sun</a>. It is also known as the "<b>Red Planet</b>", for <a href="/wiki/Mars_surface_color" title="Mars surface color">its orange-red</a> appearance.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Rees2012&#95;24-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Rees2012-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa&#95;hematite&#95;25-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa_hematite-25"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mars is a desert-like <a href="/wiki/Rocky_planet" class="mw-redirect" title="Rocky planet">rocky planet</a> with a tenuous <a href="/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Mars" title="Atmosphere of Mars">atmosphere</a> that is primarily <a href="/wiki/Carbon_dioxide" title="Carbon dioxide">carbon dioxide</a> (<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1123817410">.mw-parser-output .template-chem2-su{display:inline-block;font-size:80%;line-height:1;vertical-align:-0.35em}.mw-parser-output .template-chem2-su>span{display:block;text-align:left}.mw-parser-output sub.template-chem2-sub{font-size:80%;vertical-align:-0.35em}.mw-parser-output sup.template-chem2-sup{font-size:80%;vertical-align:0.65em}</style><span class="chemf nowrap">CO<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span>). At the average surface level the atmospheric pressure is a few thousandths of <a href="/wiki/Earth" title="Earth">Earth</a>'s, atmospheric temperature ranges from −153 to 20&#160;°C (−243 to 68&#160;°F),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-p167&#95;26-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-p167-26"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Cosmic_radiation" class="mw-redirect" title="Cosmic radiation">cosmic radiation</a> is high. Mars retains some water, <a href="/wiki/Groundwater_on_Mars" title="Groundwater on Mars">in the ground</a> as well as thinly in the atmosphere, forming <a href="/wiki/Cirrus_cloud" title="Cirrus cloud">cirrus clouds</a>, <a href="/wiki/Fog" title="Fog">fog</a>, <a href="/wiki/Frost" title="Frost">frost</a>, larger polar regions of <a href="/wiki/Permafrost" title="Permafrost">permafrost</a> and <a href="/wiki/Martian_polar_ice_caps" title="Martian polar ice caps">ice caps</a> (with seasonal <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1123817410" /><span class="chemf nowrap">CO<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span> snow), but no bodies of liquid surface water. Its surface gravity is roughly a third of Earth's or double that of the Moon. Its diameter, 6,779&#160;km (4,212&#160;mi), is about <a href="/wiki/List_of_Solar_System_objects_by_size" title="List of Solar System objects by size">half the Earth</a>'s, or twice the Moon's, and its surface area is the size of all the dry land of Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Martian_regolith#Atmospheric_dust" title="Martian regolith">Fine dust</a> is prevalent across the surface and the atmosphere, being picked up and spread at the low Martian gravity even by the weak wind of the tenuous atmosphere.
The terrain of Mars roughly follows a north–south divide, the <a href="/wiki/Martian_dichotomy" title="Martian dichotomy">Martian dichotomy</a>, with the northern hemisphere mainly consisting of relatively flat, low lying <a href="/wiki/Plain" title="Plain">plains</a>, and the southern hemisphere of cratered <a href="/wiki/Highland" title="Highland">highlands</a>. <a href="/wiki/Geology_of_Mars" title="Geology of Mars">Geologically</a>, the planet is fairly active with <a href="/wiki/Marsquake" title="Marsquake">marsquakes</a> trembling underneath the ground, but also hosts many enormous <a href="/wiki/Volcano" title="Volcano">volcanoes</a> that are extinct (the tallest is <a href="/wiki/Olympus_Mons" title="Olympus Mons">Olympus Mons</a>, 21.9&#160;km or 13.6&#160;mi tall), as well as one of the largest <a href="/wiki/Canyon" title="Canyon">canyons</a> in the Solar System (<a href="/wiki/Valles_Marineris" title="Valles Marineris">Valles Marineris</a>, 4,000&#160;km or 2,500&#160;mi long). Mars has <a href="/wiki/Moons_of_Mars" title="Moons of Mars">two natural satellites</a> that are small and irregular in shape: <a href="/wiki/Phobos_(moon)" title="Phobos (moon)">Phobos</a> and <a href="/wiki/Deimos_(moon)" title="Deimos (moon)">Deimos</a>. With a significant <a href="/wiki/Axial_tilt" title="Axial tilt">axial tilt</a> of 25&#160;degrees, Mars experiences <a href="/wiki/Season" title="Season">seasons</a>, like Earth (which has an axial tilt of 23.5&#160;degrees). A <a href="/wiki/Timekeeping_on_Mars" title="Timekeeping on Mars">Martian solar year</a> is equal to 1.88&#160;Earth years (687&#160;Earth days), a Martian solar day (<a href="/wiki/Mars_sol" title="Mars sol">sol</a>) is equal to 24.6&#160;hours.
</p><p>Mars formed along with the other planets approximately 4.5&#160;billion years ago. During the martian <a href="/wiki/Noachian" title="Noachian">Noachian</a> period (4.5 to 3.5&#160;billion years ago), its surface was marked by <a href="/wiki/Impact_event" title="Impact event">meteor impacts</a>, valley formation, erosion, the <a href="/wiki/Mars_ocean_theory" class="mw-redirect" title="Mars ocean theory">possible presence of water oceans</a> and the loss of its <a href="/wiki/Magnetosphere" title="Magnetosphere">magnetosphere</a>. The <a href="/wiki/Hesperian" title="Hesperian">Hesperian</a> period (beginning 3.5&#160;billion years ago and ending 3.3–2.9&#160;billion years ago) was dominated by widespread <a href="/wiki/Volcanism" title="Volcanism">volcanic activity</a> and flooding that carved immense <a href="/wiki/Outflow_channels" title="Outflow channels">outflow channels</a>. The <a href="/wiki/Amazonian_(Mars)" title="Amazonian (Mars)">Amazonian</a> period, which continues to the present, is the currently dominating and remaining <a href="/wiki/Aeolian_processes" title="Aeolian processes">influence on geological processes</a>. Because of Mars's geological history, the possibility of past or present <a href="/wiki/Life_on_Mars" title="Life on Mars">life on Mars</a> remains an area of active scientific investigation, with some possible traces needing further examination.
</p><p>Being <a href="/wiki/Apparent_magnitude" title="Apparent magnitude">visible</a> with the naked eye in Earth's sky as a red <a href="/wiki/Classical_planet" title="Classical planet">wandering star</a>, Mars has been <a href="/wiki/History_of_Mars_observation" title="History of Mars observation">observed throughout history</a>, acquiring diverse associations in different cultures. In 1963 the first flight to Mars took place with <a href="/wiki/Mars_1" title="Mars 1">Mars 1</a>, but communication was lost en route. The first successful flyby <a href="/wiki/Exploration_of_Mars" title="Exploration of Mars">exploration of Mars</a> was conducted in 1965 with <a href="/wiki/Mariner_4" title="Mariner 4">Mariner 4</a>. In 1971 <a href="/wiki/Mariner_9" title="Mariner 9">Mariner 9</a> entered orbit around Mars, being the first spacecraft to orbit any body other than the Moon, Sun or Earth; following in the same year were the first uncontrolled impact (<a href="/wiki/Mars_2" title="Mars 2">Mars 2</a>) and first successful landing (<a href="/wiki/Mars_3" title="Mars 3">Mars 3</a>) on Mars. Probes have been active on Mars continuously since 1997. At times, more than ten <a href="/wiki/List_of_missions_to_Mars" title="List of missions to Mars">probes have simultaneously operated</a> in orbit or on the surface, more than at any other planet beyond Earth. Mars is an often proposed target for future <a href="/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars" title="Human mission to Mars">crewed exploration missions</a>, though no such mission is currently planned.
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc" />
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Natural_history">Natural history</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Geological_history_of_Mars" title="Geological history of Mars">Geological history of Mars</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Formation">Formation</h3></div>
<p>Scientists have theorized that during the <a href="/wiki/Formation_and_evolution_of_the_Solar_System" title="Formation and evolution of the Solar System">Solar System's formation</a>, Mars was created as the result of a <a href="/wiki/Stochastic_process" title="Stochastic process">random process</a> of run-away <a href="/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics)" title="Accretion (astrophysics)">accretion</a> of material from the <a href="/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk" title="Protoplanetary disk">protoplanetary disk</a> that orbited the Sun. Mars has many distinctive chemical features caused by its position in the Solar System. Elements with comparatively low boiling points, such as <a href="/wiki/Chlorine" title="Chlorine">chlorine</a>, <a href="/wiki/Phosphorus" title="Phosphorus">phosphorus</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Sulfur" title="Sulfur">sulfur</a>, are much more common on Mars than on Earth; these elements were probably pushed outward by the young Sun's energetic <a href="/wiki/Solar_wind" title="Solar wind">solar wind</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ssr96&#95;1&#95;4&#95;197&#95;28-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ssr96_1_4_197-28"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Late_Heavy_Bombardment">Late Heavy Bombardment</h3></div>
<p>After the formation of the planets, the inner Solar System may have been subjected to the so-called <a href="/wiki/Late_Heavy_Bombardment" title="Late Heavy Bombardment">Late Heavy Bombardment</a>. About 60% of the surface of Mars shows a record of impacts from that era,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zharkov93&#95;29-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zharkov93-29"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-icarus165&#95;1&#95;30-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-icarus165_1-30"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-barlow88&#95;31-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-barlow88-31"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> whereas much of the remaining surface is probably underlain by immense impact basins caused by those events. However, more recent modeling has disputed the existence of the Late Heavy Bombardment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-32" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-32"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There is evidence of an enormous impact basin in the Northern Hemisphere of Mars, spanning 10,600 by 8,500 kilometres (6,600 by 5,300&#160;mi), or roughly four times the size of the Moon's <a href="/wiki/South_Pole%E2%80%93Aitken_basin" title="South Pole–Aitken basin">South Pole–Aitken basin</a>, which would be the largest impact basin yet discovered if confirmed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-northcratersn&#95;33-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-northcratersn-33"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It has been hypothesized that the basin was formed when Mars was struck by a <a href="/wiki/Pluto" title="Pluto">Pluto</a>-sized body about four billion years ago. The event, thought to be the cause of the <a href="/wiki/Martian_dichotomy" title="Martian dichotomy">Martian hemispheric dichotomy</a>, created the smooth <a href="/wiki/Borealis_basin" class="mw-redirect" title="Borealis basin">Borealis basin</a> that covers 40% of the planet.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sciam080627&#95;34-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sciam080627-34"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nyt080626&#95;35-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nyt080626-35"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A 2023 study shows evidence, based on the <a href="/wiki/Orbital_inclination" title="Orbital inclination">orbital inclination</a> of <a href="/wiki/Deimos_(moon)" title="Deimos (moon)">Deimos</a> (a small <a href="/wiki/Natural_satellite" title="Natural satellite">moon</a> of Mars), that Mars may once have had a <a href="/wiki/Ring_system" title="Ring system">ring system</a> 3.5&#160;billion years to 4&#160;billion years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-36" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-36"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This ring system may have been formed from a moon, 20 times more <a href="/wiki/Mass" title="Mass">massive</a> than <a href="/wiki/Phobos_(moon)" title="Phobos (moon)">Phobos</a>, orbiting Mars billions of years ago; and Phobos would be a remnant of that ring.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-38" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Geological_periods">Geological periods</h3></div>
<div class="timeline-wrapper"><map name="timeline_qet6aufoz6py1mu8mqq7qxqakgkq7df"><area href="/wiki/Pre%2DNoachian" coords="-1,9,101,30" title="Pre-Noachian" alt="Pre-Noachian" /><area href="/wiki/Noachian" coords="80,9,161,30" title="Noachian" alt="Noachian" /><area href="/wiki/Hesperian" coords="171,9,257,30" title="Hesperian" alt="Hesperian" /><area href="/wiki/Amazonian_(Mars)" coords="485,9,571,30" title="Amazonian (Mars)" alt="Amazonian (Mars)" /></map><img usemap="#timeline_qet6aufoz6py1mu8mqq7qxqakgkq7df" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/qet6aufoz6py1mu8mqq7qxqakgkq7df.png" /></div>
<div class="center"><small>Martian time periods (millions of years ago)</small></div>
<p>Epochs:
</p>
<div class="timeline-wrapper"><map name="timeline_l9mdbu2kk36h4dwzxwhwkvq47h8t0cb"></map><img usemap="#timeline_l9mdbu2kk36h4dwzxwhwkvq47h8t0cb" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/l9mdbu2kk36h4dwzxwhwkvq47h8t0cb.png" /></div>
<p>The geological history of Mars can be split into many periods, but the following are the three primary periods:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jog91&#95;39-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jog91-39"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ssr&#95;96&#95;1&#95;4&#95;40-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ssr_96_1_4-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul><li><a href="/wiki/Noachian" title="Noachian">Noachian</a> period: Formation of the oldest extant surfaces of Mars, 4.5 to 3.5&#160;billion years ago. Noachian age surfaces are scarred by many large impact craters. The <a href="/wiki/Tharsis" title="Tharsis">Tharsis</a> bulge, a volcanic upland, is thought to have formed during this period, with extensive flooding by liquid water late in the period. Named after <a href="/wiki/Noachis_Terra" title="Noachis Terra">Noachis Terra</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:0&#95;41-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Hesperian" title="Hesperian">Hesperian</a> period: 3.5 to between 3.3 and 2.9&#160;billion years ago. The Hesperian period is marked by the formation of extensive lava plains. Named after <a href="/wiki/Hesperia_Planum" title="Hesperia Planum">Hesperia Planum</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:0&#95;41-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Amazonian_(Mars)" title="Amazonian (Mars)">Amazonian</a> period: between 3.3 and 2.9&#160;billion years ago to the present. Amazonian regions have few <a href="/wiki/Impact_event" title="Impact event">meteorite impact</a> craters but are otherwise quite varied. <a href="/wiki/Olympus_Mons" title="Olympus Mons">Olympus Mons</a> formed during this period, with lava flows elsewhere on Mars. Named after <a href="/wiki/Amazonis_Planitia" title="Amazonis Planitia">Amazonis Planitia</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:0&#95;41-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Recent_geological_activity">Recent geological activity</h3></div>
<p>Geological activity is still taking place on Mars. The <a href="/wiki/Athabasca_Valles" title="Athabasca Valles">Athabasca Valles</a> is home to sheet-like lava flows created about 200&#160;million years ago. Water flows in the <a href="/wiki/Graben" title="Graben">grabens</a> called the <a href="/wiki/Cerberus_Fossae" title="Cerberus Fossae">Cerberus Fossae</a> occurred less than 20&#160;million years ago, indicating equally recent volcanic intrusions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ag44&#95;4&#95;42-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ag44_4-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <i><a href="/wiki/Mars_Reconnaissance_Orbiter" title="Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter">Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter</a></i> has captured images of avalanches.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-43" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Physical_characteristics">Physical characteristics</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Geology_of_Mars" title="Geology of Mars">Geology of Mars</a> and <a href="/wiki/Composition_of_Mars" title="Composition of Mars">Composition of Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Terrestrial_planet_size_comp_2024.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Terrestrial_planet_size_comp_2024.png/500px-Terrestrial_planet_size_comp_2024.png" decoding="async" width="350" height="147" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Terrestrial_planet_size_comp_2024.png/960px-Terrestrial_planet_size_comp_2024.png 2x" data-file-width="1024" data-file-height="431" /></a><figcaption>Mars depicted to scale alongside the <a href="/wiki/Planetary-mass_object" title="Planetary-mass object">planetary-mass objects</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Inner_Solar_System" class="mw-redirect" title="Inner Solar System">Inner Solar System</a>. From left: <a href="/wiki/Mercury_(planet)" title="Mercury (planet)">Mercury</a>, <a href="/wiki/Venus" title="Venus">Venus</a>, <a href="/wiki/Earth" title="Earth">Earth</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Moon" title="Moon">Moon</a>, Mars and <a href="/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet)" title="Ceres (dwarf planet)">Ceres</a>.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mars is approximately half the diameter of Earth or twice that of the Moon, with a surface area only slightly less than the total area of Earth's dry land.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NasaFactSheet&#95;3-12" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NasaFactSheet-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mars is less dense than Earth, having about 15% of Earth's volume and 11% of Earth's <a href="/wiki/Mass" title="Mass">mass</a>, resulting in about 38% of <a href="/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth" title="Gravity of Earth">Earth's surface gravity</a>. Mars is the only presently known example of a <a href="/wiki/Desert_planet" title="Desert planet">desert planet</a>, a rocky planet with a surface akin to that of Earth's <a href="/wiki/Desert" title="Desert">deserts</a>. The red-orange appearance of the Martian surface is caused by <a href="/wiki/Iron(III)_oxide" title="Iron(III) oxide">iron(III) oxide</a> (<a href="/wiki/Nanophase" class="mw-redirect" title="Nanophase">nanophase</a> Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) and the <a href="/wiki/Iron(III)_oxide-hydroxide" title="Iron(III) oxide-hydroxide">iron(III) oxide-hydroxide</a> mineral <a href="/wiki/Goethite" title="Goethite">goethite</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-rust&#95;45-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-rust-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It can look like <a href="/wiki/Butterscotch" title="Butterscotch">butterscotch</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ismars&#95;46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ismars-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> other common surface colors include golden, brown, tan, and greenish, depending on the <a href="/wiki/Mineral" title="Mineral">minerals</a> present.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ismars&#95;46-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ismars-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Internal_structure">Internal structure</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_Internal_Structure_(2024).svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Mars_Internal_Structure_%282024%29.svg/500px-Mars_Internal_Structure_%282024%29.svg.png" decoding="async" width="340" height="255" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Mars_Internal_Structure_%282024%29.svg/960px-Mars_Internal_Structure_%282024%29.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="2048" data-file-height="1536" /></a><figcaption>Internal structure of Mars<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Stähler&#95;47-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Stähler-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023Natur.622..712S&#95;48-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023Natur.622..712S-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_0" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm/250px--GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="141" data-durationhint="89" data-mwtitle="GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ae/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="854" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ae/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm.1080p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="1080p.vp9.webm" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ae/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;av01.0.09M.08, opus&quot;" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ae/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="256" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ae/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm/GMM-3_Mars_Gravity.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="426" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Mapped gravity field of Mars</figcaption></figure>
<p>Like Earth, Mars is <a href="/wiki/Planetary_differentiation" title="Planetary differentiation">differentiated</a> into a dense metallic <a href="/wiki/Planetary_core" title="Planetary core">core</a> overlaid by less dense rocky layers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nimmo&#95;2005&#95;49-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nimmo_2005-49"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-mars-in-depth&#95;50-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mars-in-depth-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The outermost layer is the crust, which is on average about 42–56 kilometres (26–35&#160;mi) thick,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023GeoRL..5003482K&#95;51-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023GeoRL..5003482K-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> with a minimum thickness of 6 kilometres (3.7&#160;mi) in <a href="/wiki/Isidis_Planitia" title="Isidis Planitia">Isidis Planitia</a>, and a maximum thickness of 117 kilometres (73&#160;mi) in the southern <a href="/wiki/Tharsis" title="Tharsis">Tharsis plateau</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2022JGRE..12707298W&#95;52-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2022JGRE..12707298W-52"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For comparison, Earth's crust averages 27.3 ± 4.8&#160;km in thickness.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2013GGG....14.2003H&#95;53-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2013GGG....14.2003H-53"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The most abundant elements in the Martian <a href="/wiki/Crust_(geology)" title="Crust (geology)">crust</a> are <a href="/wiki/Silicon" title="Silicon">silicon</a>, <a href="/wiki/Oxygen" title="Oxygen">oxygen</a>, <a href="/wiki/Iron" title="Iron">iron</a>, <a href="/wiki/Magnesium" title="Magnesium">magnesium</a>, <a href="/wiki/Aluminium" title="Aluminium">aluminum</a>, <a href="/wiki/Calcium" title="Calcium">calcium</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Potassium" title="Potassium">potassium</a>. Mars is confirmed to be seismically active;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023GeoRL..5003619F&#95;54-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023GeoRL..5003619F-54"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> in 2019, it was reported that <a href="/wiki/InSight" title="InSight">InSight</a> had detected and recorded over 450 <a href="/wiki/Marsquake" title="Marsquake">marsquakes</a> and related events.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2020NatCo..11.1014G&#95;55-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2020NatCo..11.1014G-55"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-2020NatGe..13..183B&#95;56-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2020NatGe..13..183B-56"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Beneath the crust is a silicate <a href="/wiki/Mantle_(geology)" title="Mantle (geology)">mantle</a> responsible for many of the <a href="/wiki/Tectonics_of_Mars" title="Tectonics of Mars">tectonic</a> and <a href="/wiki/Volcanology_of_Mars" class="mw-redirect" title="Volcanology of Mars">volcanic features</a> on the planet's surface. The upper Martian mantle is a <a href="/wiki/Low-velocity_zone" title="Low-velocity zone">low-velocity zone</a>, where the velocity of seismic waves is lower than surrounding depth intervals. The mantle appears to be rigid down to the depth of about 250&#160;km,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023Natur.622..712S&#95;48-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023Natur.622..712S-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> giving Mars a very thick <a href="/wiki/Lithosphere" title="Lithosphere">lithosphere</a> compared to Earth. Below this the mantle gradually becomes more ductile, and the seismic wave velocity starts to grow again.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Khan2021&#95;57-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Khan2021-57"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Martian mantle does not appear to have a thermally insulating layer analogous to Earth's <a href="/wiki/Lower_mantle" title="Lower mantle">lower mantle</a>; instead, below 1050&#160;km in depth, it becomes mineralogically similar to Earth's <a href="/wiki/Transition_zone_(Earth)" title="Transition zone (Earth)">transition zone</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Stähler&#95;47-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Stähler-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At the bottom of the mantle lies a basal <a href="/wiki/Magma_ocean" title="Magma ocean">liquid silicate layer</a> approximately 150–180&#160;km thick.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023Natur.622..712S&#95;48-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023Natur.622..712S-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023Natur.622..718K&#95;58-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023Natur.622..718K-58"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Martian mantle appears to be highly heterogenous, with dense fragments up to 4 km across, likely injected deep into the planet by colossal impacts ~4.5 billion years ago; high-frequency waves from eight marsquakes slowed as they passed these localized regions, and modeling indicates the heterogeneities are compositionally distinct debris preserved because Mars lacks plate tectonics and has a sluggishly convecting interior that prevents complete homogenization.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Charalambous2025&#95;59-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Charalambous2025-59"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-60" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-60"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>58<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Mars's <a href="/wiki/Iron%E2%80%93nickel_alloy" title="Iron–nickel alloy">iron and nickel</a> core is at least partially molten, and may have a solid inner core.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023Natur.619..733L&#95;61-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023Natur.619..733L-61"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ArsTechnicaOn2023Natur.619..733L&#95;62-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ArsTechnicaOn2023Natur.619..733L-62"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bi2025&#95;63-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bi2025-63"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is around half of Mars's radius, approximately 1650–1675&#160;km, and is enriched in light elements such as <a href="/wiki/Sulfur" title="Sulfur">sulfur</a>, oxygen, <a href="/wiki/Carbon" title="Carbon">carbon</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Hydrogen" title="Hydrogen">hydrogen</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-10.1038/d41586-023-03151-x&#95;64-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10.1038/d41586-023-03151-x-64"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-10.1038/d41586-023-03271-4&#95;65-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10.1038/d41586-023-03271-4-65"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The temperature of the core is estimated to be 2000–2400&#160;K,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-66" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-66"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> compared to 5400–6230 K for <a href="/wiki/Earth%27s_inner_core" title="Earth&#39;s inner core">Earth's solid inner core</a>. Whether or not a solid inner core is present inside Mars remains a topic of active research; a 2023 study using data from the <a href="/wiki/InSight" title="InSight">InSight</a> lander suggested the absence of a solid inner core,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2023Natur.619..733L&#95;61-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2023Natur.619..733L-61"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> however a 2025 study based on the same data reported the detection of a solid inner core 613 kilometres (381&#160;mi) ± 67 kilometres (42&#160;mi) in radius.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bi2025&#95;63-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bi2025-63"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Surface_geology">Surface geology</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Martian_regolith" title="Martian regolith">Martian regolith</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_1" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Mars_360_animation.webm/250px--Mars_360_animation.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="250" data-durationhint="25" data-mwtitle="Mars_360_animation.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Mars_360_animation.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/04/Mars_360_animation.webm/Mars_360_animation.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="480" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/04/Mars_360_animation.webm/Mars_360_animation.webm.1080p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="1080p.vp9.webm" data-width="1080" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/04/Mars_360_animation.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9&quot;" data-width="4000" data-height="4000" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/04/Mars_360_animation.webm/Mars_360_animation.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="144" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/04/Mars_360_animation.webm/Mars_360_animation.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="240" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Mars 360 animation of the <a href="/wiki/Martian_surface" title="Martian surface">Martian surface</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Mars is a <a href="/wiki/Terrestrial_planet" title="Terrestrial planet">terrestrial planet</a> with a surface that consists of minerals containing <a href="/wiki/Silicon" title="Silicon">silicon</a> and oxygen, <a href="/wiki/Metal" title="Metal">metals</a>, and other elements that typically make up <a href="/wiki/Rock_(geology)" title="Rock (geology)">rock</a>. The <a href="/wiki/Martian_surface" title="Martian surface">Martian surface</a> is primarily composed of <a href="/wiki/Tholeiitic_magma_series" title="Tholeiitic magma series">tholeiitic</a> <a href="/wiki/Basalt" title="Basalt">basalt</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-science324&#95;5928&#95;736&#95;67-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-science324_5928_736-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> although parts are more <a href="/wiki/Silica" class="mw-redirect" title="Silica">silica</a>-rich than typical basalt and may be similar to <a href="/wiki/Andesitic" class="mw-redirect" title="Andesitic">andesitic</a> rocks on Earth, or silica glass. Regions of low <a href="/wiki/Albedo" title="Albedo">albedo</a> suggest concentrations of <a href="/wiki/Plagioclase_feldspar" class="mw-redirect" title="Plagioclase feldspar">plagioclase feldspar</a>, with northern low albedo regions displaying higher than normal concentrations of sheet silicates and high-silicon glass. Parts of the southern highlands include detectable amounts of high-calcium <a href="/wiki/Pyroxene" title="Pyroxene">pyroxenes</a>. Localized concentrations of <a href="/wiki/Hematite" title="Hematite">hematite</a> and <a href="/wiki/Olivine" title="Olivine">olivine</a> have been found.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jgr107&#95;E6&#95;68-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jgr107_E6-68"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Much of the surface is deeply covered by finely grained <a href="/wiki/Iron(III)_oxide" title="Iron(III) oxide">iron(III) oxide</a> dust.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sci300a&#95;69-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sci300a-69"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <i><a href="/wiki/Phoenix_(spacecraft)" title="Phoenix (spacecraft)">Phoenix</a></i> lander returned data showing Martian soil to be slightly alkaline and containing elements such as <a href="/wiki/Magnesium" title="Magnesium">magnesium</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sodium" title="Sodium">sodium</a>, <a href="/wiki/Potassium" title="Potassium">potassium</a> and <a href="/wiki/Chlorine" title="Chlorine">chlorine</a>. These nutrients are found in soils on Earth, and are necessary for plant growth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc080627&#95;70-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc080627-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Experiments performed by the lander showed that the Martian soil has a <a href="/wiki/Base_(chemistry)" title="Base (chemistry)">basic</a> <a href="/wiki/PH" title="PH">pH</a> of 7.7, and contains 0.6% <a href="/wiki/Perchlorate" title="Perchlorate">perchlorate</a> by weight,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-71" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-71"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-72" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-72"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> concentrations that are <a href="/wiki/Martian_soil#Toxicity" class="mw-redirect" title="Martian soil">toxic to humans</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-toxicmars&#95;73-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-toxicmars-73"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-toxicsoil1&#95;74-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-toxicsoil1-74"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>72<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Dark_slope_streak" title="Dark slope streak">Streaks</a> are common across Mars and new ones appear frequently on steep slopes of craters, troughs, and valleys. The streaks are dark at first and get lighter with age. The streaks can start in a tiny area, then spread out for hundreds of metres. They have been seen to follow the edges of boulders and other obstacles in their path. The commonly accepted hypotheses include that they are dark underlying layers of soil revealed after avalanches of bright dust or <a href="/wiki/Dust_devil" title="Dust devil">dust devils</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jpl&#95;dust&#95;devil&#95;75-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jpl_dust_devil-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Several other explanations have been put forward, including those that involve water or even the growth of organisms.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-gpl29&#95;23&#95;41&#95;76-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-gpl29_23_41-76"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-oleb33&#95;4&#95;515&#95;77-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-oleb33_4_515-77"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Environmental_radioactivity" title="Environmental radioactivity">Environmental radiation</a> levels on the surface are on average 0.64&#160;<a href="/wiki/Sievert" title="Sievert">millisieverts</a> of radiation per day, and significantly less than the radiation of&#160;1.84&#160;millisieverts per day or 22&#160;<a href="/wiki/Rad_(radiation_unit)" title="Rad (radiation unit)">millirads</a> per day during the flight to and from Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Williams&#95;2016&#95;78-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Williams_2016-78"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Wall&#95;2013&#95;79-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Wall_2013-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>77<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For comparison the radiation levels in <a href="/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit" title="Low Earth orbit">low Earth orbit</a>, where Earth's <a href="/wiki/Space_station" title="Space station">space stations</a> orbit, are around 0.5&#160;millisieverts of radiation per day.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Jet&#95;Propulsion&#95;Laboratory&#95;(JPL)&#95;2003&#95;80-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory_(JPL)_2003-80"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Hellas_Planitia" title="Hellas Planitia">Hellas Planitia</a> has the lowest surface radiation at about 0.342 millisieverts per day, featuring <a href="/wiki/Lava_tube" title="Lava tube">lava tubes</a> southwest of <a href="/wiki/Hadriacus_Mons" title="Hadriacus Mons">Hadriacus Mons</a> with potentially levels as low as 0.064 millisieverts per day,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Paris&#95;Davies&#95;Tognetti&#95;Zahniser&#95;2020&#95;81-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Paris_Davies_Tognetti_Zahniser_2020-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> comparable to radiation levels during flights on Earth.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Magnetic_characteristics">Magnetic characteristics</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Magnetic_field_of_Mars" title="Magnetic field of Mars">Magnetic field of Mars</a></div>
<p>Although Mars has no evidence of a structured <a href="/wiki/Magnetic_field_of_Mars" title="Magnetic field of Mars">global magnetic field</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-magnetosphere&#95;82-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-magnetosphere-82"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> observations show that parts of the planet's crust have been magnetized, suggesting that alternating polarity reversals of its dipole field have occurred in the past. This <a href="/wiki/Paleomagnetism" title="Paleomagnetism">paleomagnetism</a> of magnetically susceptible minerals is similar to the <a href="/wiki/Plate_tectonics#Magnetic_striping" title="Plate tectonics">alternating bands found on Earth's ocean floors</a>. One hypothesis, published in 1999 and re-examined in October&#160;2005 (with the help of the <i><a href="/wiki/Mars_Global_Surveyor" title="Mars Global Surveyor">Mars Global Surveyor</a></i>), is that these bands suggest <a href="/wiki/Plate_tectonics" title="Plate tectonics">plate tectonic activity</a> on Mars four billion years ago, before the planetary <a href="/wiki/Dynamo_theory" title="Dynamo theory">dynamo</a> ceased to function and the planet's magnetic field faded.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-plates&#95;83-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-plates-83"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Geography_and_features">Geography and features</h2></div>
<p><span class="anchor" id="Geography"></span>
</p>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Geography_of_Mars" class="mw-redirect" title="Geography of Mars">Geography of Mars</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Areoid" class="mw-redirect" title="Areoid">Areoid</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Category:Surface_features_of_Mars" title="Category:Surface features of Mars">Category:Surface features of Mars</a></div><figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:USGS-PlanetMars-TopographicalMap.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/USGS-PlanetMars-TopographicalMap.png/500px-USGS-PlanetMars-TopographicalMap.png" decoding="async" width="400" height="183" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/USGS-PlanetMars-TopographicalMap.png/960px-USGS-PlanetMars-TopographicalMap.png 2x" data-file-width="10292" data-file-height="4720" /></a><figcaption>Topographical map of Mars with features labeled and the <a href="/wiki/Martian_dichotomy" title="Martian dichotomy">Martian dichotomy</a> visible (northern low lying and southern highland hemispheres)</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_surface_map.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Mars_surface_map.png/250px-Mars_surface_map.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Mars_surface_map.png/500px-Mars_surface_map.png 2x" data-file-width="9600" data-file-height="5400" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Martian_surface" title="Martian surface">Mars surface</a> map rendered using a color map and a <a href="/wiki/Heightmap" title="Heightmap">heightmap</a> from <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Although better remembered for mapping the Moon, <a href="/wiki/Johann_Heinrich_von_M%C3%A4dler" title="Johann Heinrich von Mädler">Johann Heinrich von Mädler</a> and <a href="/wiki/Wilhelm_Beer" title="Wilhelm Beer">Wilhelm Beer</a> were the first areographers. They began by establishing that most of Mars's surface features were permanent and by more precisely determining the planet's rotation period. In 1840, Mädler combined ten years of observations and drew the first map of Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-84" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-84"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Features on Mars are named from a variety of sources. <a href="/wiki/Classical_albedo_features_on_Mars" title="Classical albedo features on Mars">Albedo features</a> are named for classical mythology. Craters larger than roughly 50&#160;km are named for deceased scientists and writers and others who have contributed to the study of Mars. Smaller craters are named for towns and villages of the world with populations of less than 100,000. Large valleys are named for the word "Mars" or "star" in various languages; smaller valleys are named for rivers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-usgs&#95;85-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-usgs-85"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Large <a href="/wiki/Albedo" title="Albedo">albedo</a> features retain many of the older names but are often updated to reflect new knowledge of the nature of the features. For example, <i>Nix Olympica</i> (the snows of Olympus) has become <i><a href="/wiki/Olympus_Mons" title="Olympus Mons">Olympus Mons</a></i> (Mount Olympus).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-viking&#95;1950&#95;2000&#95;86-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-viking_1950_2000-86"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The surface of Mars as seen from Earth is divided into two kinds of areas, with differing albedo. The paler plains covered with dust and sand rich in reddish iron oxides were once thought of as Martian "continents" and given names like <a href="/wiki/Arabia_Terra" title="Arabia Terra">Arabia Terra</a> (<i>land of Arabia</i>) or <a href="/wiki/Amazonis_Planitia" title="Amazonis Planitia">Amazonis Planitia</a> (<i>Amazonian plain</i>). The dark features were thought to be seas, hence their names <a href="/wiki/Mare_Erythraeum" title="Mare Erythraeum">Mare Erythraeum</a>, Mare Sirenum and <a href="/wiki/Aurorae_Sinus" title="Aurorae Sinus">Aurorae Sinus</a>. The largest dark feature seen from Earth is <a href="/wiki/Syrtis_Major_Planum" title="Syrtis Major Planum">Syrtis Major Planum</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-87" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-87"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The permanent northern polar ice cap is named <a href="/wiki/Planum_Boreum" title="Planum Boreum">Planum Boreum</a>. The southern cap is called <a href="/wiki/Planum_Australe" title="Planum Australe">Planum Australe</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:3&#95;88-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:3-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Mars's equator is defined by its rotation, but the location of its <a href="/wiki/Prime_Meridian" class="mw-redirect" title="Prime Meridian">Prime Meridian</a> was specified, as was Earth's (at <a href="/wiki/Greenwich" title="Greenwich">Greenwich</a>), by choice of an arbitrary point; Mädler and Beer selected a line for their first maps of Mars in 1830. After the spacecraft <i><a href="/wiki/Mariner_9" title="Mariner 9">Mariner 9</a></i> provided extensive imagery of Mars in 1972, a small crater (later called <a href="/wiki/Airy-0" title="Airy-0">Airy-0</a>), located in the <a href="/wiki/Sinus_Meridiani" title="Sinus Meridiani">Sinus Meridiani</a> ("Middle Bay" or "Meridian Bay"), was chosen by <a href="/wiki/Merton_E._Davies" title="Merton E. Davies">Merton E. Davies</a>, <a href="/wiki/Harold_Masursky" title="Harold Masursky">Harold Masursky</a>, and <a href="/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_de_Vaucouleurs" title="Gérard de Vaucouleurs">Gérard de Vaucouleurs</a> for the definition of 0.0° longitude to coincide with the original selection.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-89" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-archinal&#95;caplinger&#95;90-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-archinal_caplinger-90"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-91" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Because Mars has no oceans, and hence no "<a href="/wiki/Sea_level" title="Sea level">sea level</a>", a zero-elevation surface had to be selected as a reference level; this is called the <i><a href="/wiki/Areoid" class="mw-redirect" title="Areoid">areoid</a></i><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASAMola2007&#95;92-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASAMola2007-92"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> of Mars, analogous to the terrestrial <a href="/wiki/Geoid" title="Geoid">geoid</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ArdalanKarimi2009&#95;93-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ArdalanKarimi2009-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Zero altitude was defined by the height at which there is 610.5&#160;<a href="/wiki/Pascal_(unit)" title="Pascal (unit)">Pa</a> (6.105&#160;<a href="/wiki/Bar_(unit)" title="Bar (unit)">mbar</a>) of atmospheric pressure.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pers66&#95;94-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pers66-94"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This pressure corresponds to the <a href="/wiki/Triple_point" title="Triple point">triple point</a> of water, and it is about 0.6% of the sea level surface pressure on Earth (0.006 atm).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lunine99&#95;95-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lunine99-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>For mapping purposes, the <a href="/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey" title="United States Geological Survey">United States Geological Survey</a> divides the surface of Mars into <a href="/wiki/List_of_quadrangles_on_Mars" title="List of quadrangles on Mars">thirty cartographic quadrangles</a>, each named for a classical albedo feature it contains.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-96" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-96"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In April 2023, <i><a href="/wiki/The_New_York_Times" title="The New York Times">The New York Times</a></i> reported an updated global map of Mars based on images from the <a href="/wiki/Hope_Mars_Mission" class="mw-redirect" title="Hope Mars Mission">Hope spacecraft</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYT-20230415&#95;97-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT-20230415-97"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A related, but much more detailed, global Mars map was released by <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a> on 16 April 2023.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-STD-20230416&#95;98-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-STD-20230416-98"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Volcanoes">Volcanoes</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Volcanism_on_Mars" title="Volcanism on Mars">Volcanism on Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Olympus_Mons_-_ESA_Mars_Express_-_Flickr_-_Andrea_Luck.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Olympus_Mons_-_ESA_Mars_Express_-_Flickr_-_Andrea_Luck.png/330px-Olympus_Mons_-_ESA_Mars_Express_-_Flickr_-_Andrea_Luck.png" decoding="async" width="300" height="190" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Olympus_Mons_-_ESA_Mars_Express_-_Flickr_-_Andrea_Luck.png/960px-Olympus_Mons_-_ESA_Mars_Express_-_Flickr_-_Andrea_Luck.png 2x" data-file-width="6000" data-file-height="3797" /></a><figcaption>Picture of the tallest volcano on Mars, <a href="/wiki/Olympus_Mons" title="Olympus Mons">Olympus Mons</a>. It is approximately 550&#160;km (340&#160;mi) across.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The vast upland region <a href="/wiki/Tharsis" title="Tharsis">Tharsis</a> contains several massive volcanoes, which include the <a href="/wiki/Shield_volcano" title="Shield volcano">shield volcano</a> <a href="/wiki/Olympus_Mons" title="Olympus Mons">Olympus Mons</a>. The edifice is over 600&#160;km (370&#160;mi) wide.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-99" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-99"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Plescia04&#95;100-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Plescia04-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Because the mountain is so large, with complex structure at its edges, giving a definite height to it is difficult. Its local relief, from the foot of the cliffs which form its northwest margin to its peak, is over 21&#160;km (13&#160;mi),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Plescia04&#95;100-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Plescia04-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> a little over twice the height of <a href="/wiki/Mauna_Kea" title="Mauna Kea">Mauna Kea</a> as measured from its base on the ocean floor. The total elevation change from the plains of <a href="/wiki/Amazonis_Planitia" title="Amazonis Planitia">Amazonis Planitia</a>, over 1,000&#160;km (620&#160;mi) to the northwest, to the summit approaches 26&#160;km (16&#160;mi),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Comins&#95;101-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Comins-101"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>99<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> roughly three times the height of <a href="/wiki/Mount_Everest" title="Mount Everest">Mount Everest</a>, which in comparison stands at just over 8.8 kilometres (5.5&#160;mi). Consequently, Olympus Mons is either the <a href="/wiki/List_of_tallest_mountains_in_the_Solar_System" title="List of tallest mountains in the Solar System">tallest or second-tallest mountain in the Solar System</a>; the only known mountain which might be taller is the <a href="/wiki/Rheasilvia" title="Rheasilvia">Rheasilvia</a> peak on the asteroid <a href="/wiki/4_Vesta" title="4 Vesta">Vesta</a>, at 20–25&#160;km (12–16&#160;mi).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Schenk2012&#95;102-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Schenk2012-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Impact_topography">Impact topography</h3></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Martian_dichotomy" title="Martian dichotomy">dichotomy of Martian topography</a> is striking: northern plains flattened by lava flows contrast with the southern highlands, pitted and cratered by ancient impacts. It is possible that, four billion years ago, the Northern Hemisphere of Mars was struck by an object one-tenth to two-thirds the size of Earth's <a href="/wiki/Moon" title="Moon">Moon</a>. If this is the case, the Northern Hemisphere of Mars would be the site of an <a href="/wiki/Impact_crater" title="Impact crater">impact crater</a> 10,600 by 8,500 kilometres (6,600 by 5,300&#160;mi) in size, or roughly the area of Europe, Asia, and Australia combined, surpassing <a href="/wiki/Utopia_Planitia" title="Utopia Planitia">Utopia Planitia</a> and the Moon's <a href="/wiki/South_Pole%E2%80%93Aitken_basin" title="South Pole–Aitken basin">South Pole–Aitken basin</a> as the largest impact crater in the Solar System.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-103" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-103"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:6&#95;104-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:6-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-105" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-105"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Mars is scarred by 43,000 impact craters with a diameter of 5 kilometres (3.1&#160;mi) or greater.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-wright03&#95;106-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-wright03-106"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The largest exposed crater is <a href="/wiki/Hellas_Planitia" title="Hellas Planitia">Hellas</a>, which is 2,300 kilometres (1,400&#160;mi) wide and 7,000 metres (23,000&#160;ft) deep, and is a light <a href="/wiki/Albedo_feature" title="Albedo feature">albedo feature</a> clearly visible from Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-107" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-107"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-108" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-108"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There are other notable impact features, such as <a href="/wiki/Argyre_Planitia" title="Argyre Planitia">Argyre</a>, which is around 1,800 kilometres (1,100&#160;mi) in diameter,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-109" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Isidis_Planitia" title="Isidis Planitia">Isidis</a>, which is around 1,500 kilometres (930&#160;mi) in diameter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-110" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-110"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Due to the smaller mass and size of Mars, the probability of an object colliding with the planet is about half that of Earth. Mars is located closer to the <a href="/wiki/Asteroid_belt" title="Asteroid belt">asteroid belt</a>, so it has an increased chance of being struck by materials from that source. Mars is more likely to be struck by short-period <a href="/wiki/Comet" title="Comet">comets</a>, <i>i.e.</i>, those that lie within the orbit of <a href="/wiki/Jupiter" title="Jupiter">Jupiter</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-emp9&#95;111-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-emp9-111"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Martian craters can<sup class="noprint selfreference">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Talk:Mars" title="Talk:Mars">discuss</a></i>&#93;</sup> have a morphology that suggests the ground became wet after the meteor impact.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-emp45&#95;112-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-emp45-112"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Tectonic_sites">Tectonic sites</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_Valles_Marineris.jpeg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Mars_Valles_Marineris.jpeg/250px-Mars_Valles_Marineris.jpeg" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Mars_Valles_Marineris.jpeg/500px-Mars_Valles_Marineris.jpeg 2x" data-file-width="1552" data-file-height="1552" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Valles_Marineris" title="Valles Marineris">Valles Marineris</a>, taken by the <i><a href="/wiki/Viking_1" title="Viking 1">Viking 1</a></i> probe</figcaption></figure>
<p>The large canyon, <a href="/wiki/Valles_Marineris" title="Valles Marineris">Valles Marineris</a> (Latin for <a href="/wiki/Mariner_program" title="Mariner program">Mariner</a> Valleys, also known as Agathodaemon in the old canal maps<sup id="cite&#95;ref-113" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-113"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>), has a length of 4,000 kilometres (2,500&#160;mi) and a depth of up to 7 kilometres (4.3&#160;mi). The length of Valles Marineris is equivalent to the length of Europe and extends across one-fifth the circumference of Mars. By comparison, the <a href="/wiki/Grand_Canyon" title="Grand Canyon">Grand Canyon</a> on Earth is only 446 kilometres (277&#160;mi) long and nearly 2 kilometres (1.2&#160;mi) deep. Valles Marineris was formed due to the swelling of the Tharsis area, which caused the crust in the area of Valles Marineris to collapse. In 2012, it was proposed that Valles Marineris is not just a <a href="/wiki/Graben" title="Graben">graben</a>, but a plate boundary where 150 kilometres (93&#160;mi) of <a href="/wiki/Transform_fault" title="Transform fault">transverse motion</a> has occurred, making Mars a planet with possibly a two-<a href="/wiki/Tectonic_plate" class="mw-redirect" title="Tectonic plate">tectonic plate</a> arrangement.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tectonic&#95;114-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tectonic-114"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Lin,&#95;An&#95;115-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lin,_An-115"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>113<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Holes_and_caves">Holes and caves</h3></div>
<p>Images from the <a href="/wiki/Thermal_Emission_Imaging_System" title="Thermal Emission Imaging System">Thermal Emission Imaging System</a> (THEMIS) aboard NASA's <a href="/wiki/2001_Mars_Odyssey" title="2001 Mars Odyssey">Mars Odyssey orbiter</a> have revealed seven possible <a href="/wiki/Cave" title="Cave">cave</a> entrances on the flanks of the volcano <a href="/wiki/Arsia_Mons" title="Arsia Mons">Arsia Mons</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-cushing&#95;titus&#95;wynn07&#95;116-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cushing_titus_wynn07-116"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>114<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The caves, named after loved ones of their discoverers, are collectively known as the "seven sisters".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nau070328&#95;117-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nau070328-117"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Cave entrances measure from 100 to 252 metres (328 to 827&#160;ft) wide and they are estimated to be at least 73 to 96 metres (240 to 315&#160;ft) deep. Because light does not reach the floor of most of the caves, they may extend much deeper than these lower estimates and widen below the surface. "Dena" is the only exception; its floor is visible and was measured to be 130 metres (430&#160;ft) deep. The interiors of these caverns may be protected from micrometeoroids, UV radiation, <a href="/wiki/Solar_flare" title="Solar flare">solar flares</a> and high energy particles that bombard the planet's surface.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc070317&#95;118-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc070317-118"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>116<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-119" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-119"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Other_features">Other features</h3></div>
<div class="excerpt-block"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1066933788">.mw-parser-output .excerpt-hat .mw-editsection-like{font-style:normal}</style><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable dablink excerpt-hat selfref">This section is an excerpt from <a href="/wiki/Geysers_on_Mars" title="Geysers on Mars">Geysers on Mars</a>.<span class="mw-editsection-like plainlinks"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Geysers_on_Mars&amp;action=edit">edit</a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div><div class="excerpt">
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Geysers_on_Mars.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Geysers_on_Mars.jpg/250px-Geysers_on_Mars.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="281" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Geysers_on_Mars.jpg/500px-Geysers_on_Mars.jpg 2x" data-file-width="516" data-file-height="726" /></a><figcaption>Artist concept showing sand-laden jets erupting from Martian geysers (published by NASA; artist: <a href="/wiki/Ron_Miller_(artist_and_author)" title="Ron Miller (artist and author)">Ron Miller</a>)</figcaption></figure>
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Moc2_166b_msss.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Moc2_166b_msss.gif/250px-Moc2_166b_msss.gif" decoding="async" width="200" height="172" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Moc2_166b_msss.gif/500px-Moc2_166b_msss.gif 2x" data-file-width="615" data-file-height="530" /></a><figcaption>Dark dune spots</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Geysers_on_Mars" title="Geysers on Mars">Martian geysers</a> (or <span class="chemf nowrap">CO<span class="nowrap"><span style="display:inline-block;margin-bottom:-0.3em;vertical-align:-0.4em;line-height:1em;font-size:80%;text-align:left"><sup style="font-size:inherit;line-height:inherit;vertical-align:baseline"></sup><br /><sub style="font-size:inherit;line-height:inherit;vertical-align:baseline">2</sub></span></span></span> jets) are putative sites of small gas and dust eruptions that occur in the <a href="/wiki/Planum_Australe" title="Planum Australe">south polar region</a> of Mars during the spring thaw. "Dark dune spots" and "spiders" – or araneiforms<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Geysers&#95;on&#95;Mars&#95;Portyankina2014&#95;120-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Geysers_on_Mars_Portyankina2014-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>118<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> – are the two most visible types of features ascribed to these eruptions.
</p>
</div></div>
<div class="excerpt-block"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1066933788" /><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable dablink excerpt-hat selfref">This section is an excerpt from <a href="/wiki/Martian_regolith#Atmospheric_dust" title="Martian regolith">Martian regolith § Atmospheric dust</a>.<span class="mw-editsection-like plainlinks"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Martian_regolith&amp;action=edit">edit</a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div><div class="excerpt">
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Dust_storm_co.tif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Dust_storm_co.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Dust_storm_co.tif.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="94" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Dust_storm_co.tif/lossy-page1-500px-Dust_storm_co.tif.jpg 2x" data-file-width="9614" data-file-height="3600" /></a><figcaption>Detail of a Martian dust storm, as viewed from orbit</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:PIA03170_fig1duststroms.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/PIA03170_fig1duststroms.jpg/250px-PIA03170_fig1duststroms.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="133" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/PIA03170_fig1duststroms.jpg/500px-PIA03170_fig1duststroms.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1600" data-file-height="852" /></a><figcaption>Mars without a dust storm in June 2001 (on left) and with a global dust storm in July 2001 (on right), as seen by Mars Global Surveyor</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_2" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm/250px--PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="147" data-durationhint="99" data-mwtitle="PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/5d/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="818" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/5d/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm.1080p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm;
