codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="1080p.vp9.webm" data-width="1842" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/5d/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp8, vorbis&quot;" data-width="1920" data-height="1126" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/5d/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="246" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/5d/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm/PIA22737-Mars-2018DustStorm-MCS-MRO-Animation-20181030.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="410" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Mars dust storm in <a href="/wiki/Optical_depth" title="Optical depth">optical depth tau</a> from May to September 2018<br />(by <a href="/wiki/Mars_Climate_Sounder" class="mw-redirect" title="Mars Climate Sounder">Mars Climate Sounder</a>)</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:PIA23513-Mars-DustTower-20101130.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/PIA23513-Mars-DustTower-20101130.jpg/250px-PIA23513-Mars-DustTower-20101130.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="163" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/PIA23513-Mars-DustTower-20101130.jpg/500px-PIA23513-Mars-DustTower-20101130.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1144" data-file-height="744" /></a><figcaption>Difference of dust and water clouds: the yellow cloud at the bottom center of the image is a large dust cloud, the other white clouds are water clouds.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Similarly sized dust will settle from the thinner Martian atmosphere sooner than it would on Earth. For example, the dust suspended by the 2001 global dust storms on Mars only remained in the Martian atmosphere for 0.6 years, while the dust from <a href="/wiki/Mount_Pinatubo" title="Mount Pinatubo">Mount Pinatubo</a> took about two years to settle.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Martian&#95;regolith&#95;Cantor2001&#95;121-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Martian_regolith_Cantor2001-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, under current Martian conditions, the mass movements involved are generally much smaller than on Earth. Even the 2001 global dust storms on Mars moved only the equivalent of a very thin dust layer – about 3&#160;μm thick if deposited with uniform thickness between 58° north and south of the equator.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Martian&#95;regolith&#95;Cantor2001&#95;121-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Martian_regolith_Cantor2001-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Dust deposition at the <a href="/wiki/Mars_Exploration_Rover" title="Mars Exploration Rover">two rover sites</a> has proceeded at a rate of about the thickness of a grain every 100 <a href="/wiki/Timekeeping_on_Mars" title="Timekeeping on Mars">sols</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Martian&#95;regolith&#95;Claudin2006&#95;122-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Martian_regolith_Claudin2006-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

data-file-height="384" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption text-align-center">Dust devil on Mars as viewed by the <a href="/wiki/Curiosity_(rover)" title="Curiosity (rover)"><i>Curiosity</i> rover</a></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:186px;max-width:186px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:122px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/The_Serpent_Dust_Devil_on_Mars_PIA15116.jpg/500px-The_Serpent_Dust_Devil_on_Mars_PIA15116.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2093" data-file-height="1389" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption text-align-center">A 30 meter wide and 800 meter high dust devil. Dust devils of 20 kilometer height have been observed.</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:105px;max-width:105px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:122px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Dust_Devil_with_Labels.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Dust_Devil_with_Labels.JPG/120px-Dust_Devil_with_Labels.JPG" decoding="async" width="103" height="123" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Dust_Devil_with_Labels.JPG/250px-Dust_Devil_with_Labels.JPG 2x" data-file-width="405" data-file-height="483" /></a></span></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:165px;max-width:165px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:122px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Martian_Dust_Devil_Trails.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Martian_Dust_Devil_Trails.jpg/250px-Martian_Dust_Devil_Trails.jpg" decoding="async" width="163" height="122" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Martian_Dust_Devil_Trails.jpg/330px-Martian_Dust_Devil_Trails.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2560" data-file-height="1920" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption text-align-center">Dust devils cause twisting dark trails on the Martian surface</div></div></div></div></div>
</div></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Atmosphere">Atmosphere</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Mars" title="Atmosphere of Mars">Atmosphere of Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_-_Atmosphere_and_Cassini_Crater_-_Hope_Mission,_Orbit_19_(52252929420).png" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Image of Mars" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Mars_-_Atmosphere_and_Cassini_Crater_-_Hope_Mission%2C_Orbit_19_%2852252929420%29.png/250px-Mars_-_Atmosphere_and_Cassini_Crater_-_Hope_Mission%2C_Orbit_19_%2852252929420%29.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="193" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Mars_-_Atmosphere_and_Cassini_Crater_-_Hope_Mission%2C_Orbit_19_%2852252929420%29.png/500px-Mars_-_Atmosphere_and_Cassini_Crater_-_Hope_Mission%2C_Orbit_19_%2852252929420%29.png 2x" data-file-width="4500" data-file-height="3476" /></a><figcaption>A broad view of Mars's atmosphere by <i>Hope</i> orbiter</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mars lost its <a href="/wiki/Magnetosphere" title="Magnetosphere">magnetosphere</a> 4&#160;billion years ago,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-swind&#95;123-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-swind-123"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> possibly because of numerous asteroid strikes,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-124" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-124"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> so the <a href="/wiki/Solar_wind" title="Solar wind">solar wind</a> interacts directly with the Martian <a href="/wiki/Ionosphere" title="Ionosphere">ionosphere</a>, lowering the atmospheric density by <a href="/wiki/Atmospheric_stripping" class="mw-redirect" title="Atmospheric stripping">stripping</a> away atoms from the outer layer.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:7&#95;125-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:7-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Both <i><a href="/wiki/Mars_Global_Surveyor" title="Mars Global Surveyor">Mars Global Surveyor</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Mars_Express" title="Mars Express">Mars Express</a></i> have detected ionized atmospheric particles trailing off into space behind Mars,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-swind&#95;123-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-swind-123"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-swind2&#95;126-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-swind2-126"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and this atmospheric loss is being studied by the <a href="/wiki/MAVEN" title="MAVEN">MAVEN</a> orbiter. Compared to Earth, the <a href="/wiki/Celestial_body_atmosphere" class="mw-redirect" title="Celestial body atmosphere">atmosphere</a> of Mars is quite rarefied. <a href="/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure" title="Atmospheric pressure">Atmospheric pressure</a> on the surface today ranges from a low of 30&#160;<a href="/wiki/Pascal_(unit)" title="Pascal (unit)">Pa</a> (0.0044&#160;<a href="/wiki/Pound_per_square_inch" title="Pound per square inch">psi</a>) on <a href="/wiki/Olympus_Mons" title="Olympus Mons">Olympus Mons</a> to over 1,155&#160;Pa (0.1675&#160;psi) in <a href="/wiki/Hellas_Planitia" title="Hellas Planitia">Hellas Planitia</a>, with a mean pressure at the surface level of 600&#160;Pa (0.087&#160;psi).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bolonkin09&#95;127-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bolonkin09-127"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>125<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The highest atmospheric density on Mars is equal to that found 35 kilometres (22&#160;mi)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-atkinson07&#95;128-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-atkinson07-128"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> above Earth's surface. The resulting mean surface pressure is only 0.6% of Earth's 101.3&#160;kPa (14.69&#160;psi). The <a href="/wiki/Scale_height" title="Scale height">scale height</a> of the atmosphere is about 10.8 kilometres (6.7&#160;mi),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-carr06&#95;129-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-carr06-129"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> which is higher than Earth's 6 kilometres (3.7&#160;mi), because the surface <a href="/wiki/Gravity_of_Mars" title="Gravity of Mars">gravity of Mars</a> is only about 38% of Earth's.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-130" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-130"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>128<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The atmosphere of Mars consists of about 96% <a href="/wiki/Carbon_dioxide" title="Carbon dioxide">carbon dioxide</a>, 1.93% <a href="/wiki/Argon" title="Argon">argon</a> and 1.89% <a href="/wiki/Nitrogen" title="Nitrogen">nitrogen</a> along with traces of <a href="/wiki/Oxygen" title="Oxygen">oxygen</a> and water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NasaFactSheet&#95;3-13" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NasaFactSheet-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Abundance&#95;131-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Abundance-131"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>129<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:7&#95;125-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:7-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The atmosphere is quite dusty, containing particulates about 1.5 <a href="/wiki/%CE%9Cm" class="mw-redirect" title="Μm">μm</a> in diameter which give the <a href="/wiki/Extraterrestrial_skies#Mars" class="mw-redirect" title="Extraterrestrial skies">Martian sky</a> a <a href="/wiki/Tawny_(color)" class="mw-redirect" title="Tawny (color)">tawny</a> color when seen from the surface.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-dusty&#95;132-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dusty-132"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It may take on a <a href="/wiki/Pink" title="Pink">pink</a> hue due to <a href="/wiki/Iron_oxide" title="Iron oxide">iron oxide</a> particles suspended in it.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Rees2012&#95;24-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Rees2012-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>
Despite repeated detections of <a href="/wiki/Methane_on_Mars" class="mw-redirect" title="Methane on Mars">methane on Mars</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-133" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> there is no scientific consensus as to its origin.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-134" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-134"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>132<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> One suggestion is that methane exists on Mars and that its concentration fluctuates seasonally.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Sample2018&#95;135-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sample2018-135"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-plumes&#95;136-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-plumes-136"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>134<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nature460&#95;137-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nature460-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The existence of methane could be produced by non-biological process such as <a href="/wiki/Serpentinite" title="Serpentinite">serpentinization</a> involving water, carbon dioxide, and the mineral <a href="/wiki/Olivine" title="Olivine">olivine</a>, which is known to be common on Mars, or by Martian life.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-138" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-138"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>136<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-olivine&#95;139-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-olivine-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></p><figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:PIA18613-MarsMAVEN-Atmosphere-3UV-Views-20141014.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/PIA18613-MarsMAVEN-Atmosphere-3UV-Views-20141014.jpg/330px-PIA18613-MarsMAVEN-Atmosphere-3UV-Views-20141014.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="119" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/PIA18613-MarsMAVEN-Atmosphere-3UV-Views-20141014.jpg/960px-PIA18613-MarsMAVEN-Atmosphere-3UV-Views-20141014.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5170" data-file-height="2045" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Mars" title="Atmosphere of Mars">Escaping atmosphere</a> on Mars (<a href="/wiki/Carbon" title="Carbon">carbon</a>, <a href="/wiki/Oxygen" title="Oxygen">oxygen</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Hydrogen" title="Hydrogen">hydrogen</a>) by <a href="/wiki/MAVEN" title="MAVEN">MAVEN</a> in <a href="/wiki/Ultraviolet%E2%80%93visible_spectroscopy" title="Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy">UV</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA-20141014-NJ&#95;140-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-20141014-NJ-140"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Compared to Earth, its higher concentration of atmospheric CO<sub>2</sub> and lower surface pressure may be why sound is attenuated more on Mars, where natural sources are rare apart from the wind. Using acoustic recordings collected by the <i>Perseverance</i> rover, researchers concluded that the speed of sound there is approximately 240&#160;m/s for frequencies below 240&#160;Hz, and 250&#160;m/s for those above.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-141" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-141"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-142" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-142"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>140<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Aurora_on_Mars" title="Aurora on Mars">Auroras</a> have been detected on Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-143" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-143"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>141<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-144" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA-20150318&#95;145-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-20150318-145"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>143<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Because Mars lacks a global magnetic field, the types and distribution of auroras there differ from those on Earth;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-146" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>144<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> rather than being mostly restricted to polar regions as is the case on Earth, a Martian aurora can encompass the planet.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:8&#95;147-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:8-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In September 2017, NASA reported <a href="/wiki/Radiation" title="Radiation">radiation</a> levels on the surface of the planet Mars were temporarily <a href="/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(radiation)" title="Orders of magnitude (radiation)">doubled</a>, and were associated with an <a href="/wiki/Aurora" title="Aurora">aurora</a> 25 times brighter than any observed earlier, due to a massive, and unexpected, <a href="/wiki/Coronal_mass_ejection" title="Coronal mass ejection">solar storm</a> in the middle of the month.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:8&#95;147-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:8-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa20170929&#95;148-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa20170929-148"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Climate">Climate</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Climate_of_Mars" title="Climate of Mars">Climate of Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_Before_and_During_Global_Dust_Storm.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Mars_Before_and_During_Global_Dust_Storm.jpg/250px-Mars_Before_and_During_Global_Dust_Storm.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Mars_Before_and_During_Global_Dust_Storm.jpg/500px-Mars_Before_and_During_Global_Dust_Storm.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1280" data-file-height="720" /></a><figcaption>Mars without (on left) and with a global dust storm in July 2001 (on right), including different visible water ice cloud covers, as seen by the Hubble Space Telescope</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:PIA22546-Mars-AnnualCO2ice-N%26SPoles-20180806.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/PIA22546-Mars-AnnualCO2ice-N%26SPoles-20180806.gif/250px-PIA22546-Mars-AnnualCO2ice-N%26SPoles-20180806.gif" decoding="async" width="250" height="125" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/PIA22546-Mars-AnnualCO2ice-N%26SPoles-20180806.gif/500px-PIA22546-Mars-AnnualCO2ice-N%26SPoles-20180806.gif 2x" data-file-width="849" data-file-height="423" /></a><figcaption>Rendering of the change of <a href="/wiki/Dry_ice" title="Dry ice">CO<sub>2</sub> ice</a> (not water ice) coverage on the north (left) and south (right) poles of Mars between northern and southern summer.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mars has seasons, alternating between its northern and southern hemispheres, similar to on Earth. Additionally the orbit of Mars has, compared to Earth's, a large <a href="/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity" title="Orbital eccentricity">eccentricity</a> and approaches <a href="/wiki/Apsis" title="Apsis">perihelion</a> when it is summer in its southern hemisphere and winter in its northern, and <a href="/wiki/Apsis" title="Apsis">aphelion</a> when it is winter in its southern hemisphere and summer in its northern. As a result, the seasons in its southern hemisphere are more extreme and the seasons in its northern are milder than would otherwise be the case. The summer temperatures in the south can be warmer than the equivalent summer temperatures in the north by up to 30&#160;°C (54&#160;°F).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-goodman97&#95;149-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-goodman97-149"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Martian surface temperatures vary from lows of about −110&#160;°C (−166&#160;°F) to highs of up to 35&#160;°C (95&#160;°F) in equatorial summer.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hot&#95;18-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hot-18"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The wide range in temperatures is due to the thin atmosphere which cannot store much solar heat, the low atmospheric pressure (about 1% that of the <a href="/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Earth" title="Atmosphere of Earth">atmosphere of Earth</a>), and the low <a href="/wiki/Thermal_inertia" title="Thermal inertia">thermal inertia</a> of Martian soil.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa&#95;surface&#95;150-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa_surface-150"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The planet is 1.52 times as far from the Sun as Earth, resulting in just 43% of the amount of sunlight.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-disc920901&#95;151-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-disc920901-151"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-152" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-152"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>150<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Mars has the largest <a href="/wiki/Dust_storm" title="Dust storm">dust storms</a> in the Solar System, reaching speeds of over 160&#160;km/h (100&#160;mph). These can vary from a storm over a small area, to gigantic storms that cover the entire planet. They tend to occur when Mars is closest to the Sun, and have been shown to increase global temperature.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-philips01&#95;153-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-philips01-153"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Seasons also produce <a href="/wiki/Dry_ice" title="Dry ice">dry ice</a> covering <a href="/wiki/Martian_polar_ice_caps" title="Martian polar ice caps">polar ice caps</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-a652&#95;154-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-a652-154"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>152<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Hydrology">Hydrology</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Water_on_Mars" title="Water on Mars">Water on Mars</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Groundwater_on_Mars" title="Groundwater on Mars">Groundwater on Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:PIA26046-Mars-BuriedIceMaps-20231026.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/PIA26046-Mars-BuriedIceMaps-20231026.jpg/500px-PIA26046-Mars-BuriedIceMaps-20231026.jpg" decoding="async" width="450" height="184" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/PIA26046-Mars-BuriedIceMaps-20231026.jpg/960px-PIA26046-Mars-BuriedIceMaps-20231026.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2852" data-file-height="1169" /></a><figcaption>Mars contains water, though mostly as dust covered polar layers of ice, as mapped in this image.</figcaption></figure>
<p>While Mars contains <a href="/wiki/Water_on_terrestrial_planets_of_the_Solar_System" title="Water on terrestrial planets of the Solar System">water in larger amounts</a>, most of it is dust covered water ice at the <a href="/wiki/Martian_polar_ice_caps" title="Martian polar ice caps">Martian polar ice caps</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-specials1&#95;155-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-specials1-155"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc040124&#95;156-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc040124-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-157" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-157"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>155<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sci299&#95;158-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sci299-158"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:3&#95;88-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:3-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
The volume of water ice in the south polar ice cap, if melted, would be enough to cover most of the surface of the planet with a depth of 11 metres (36&#160;ft).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa070315&#95;159-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa070315-159"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>157<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Water in its liquid form cannot persist on the surface due to Mars's low atmospheric pressure, which is less than 1% that of Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa.gov&#95;160-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa.gov-160"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>158<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Only at the lowest of elevations are the pressure and temperature high enough for liquid water to exist for short periods.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mars-in-depth&#95;50-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mars-in-depth-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-jgr110&#95;161-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jgr110-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Although little water is present in the atmosphere, there is enough to produce clouds of water ice and different cases of <a href="/wiki/Snow" title="Snow">snow</a> and frost, often mixed with snow of <a href="/wiki/Carbon_dioxide" title="Carbon dioxide">carbon dioxide</a> <a href="/wiki/Dry_ice" title="Dry ice">dry ice</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-k104&#95;162-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-k104-162"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Past_hydrosphere">Past hydrosphere</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Artist%E2%80%99s_impression_of_Mars_four_billion_years_ago.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Artist%E2%80%99s_impression_of_Mars_four_billion_years_ago.jpg/250px-Artist%E2%80%99s_impression_of_Mars_four_billion_years_ago.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="182" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Artist%E2%80%99s_impression_of_Mars_four_billion_years_ago.jpg/500px-Artist%E2%80%99s_impression_of_Mars_four_billion_years_ago.jpg 2x" data-file-width="6970" data-file-height="5072" /></a><figcaption>Artist's impression of Mars four billion years ago</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Geomorphology" title="Geomorphology">Landforms</a> visible on Mars strongly suggest that liquid water has existed on the planet's surface. Huge linear swathes of scoured ground, known as <a href="/wiki/Outflow_channels" title="Outflow channels">outflow channels</a>, cut across the surface in about 25 places. These are thought to be a record of erosion caused by the catastrophic release of water from subsurface aquifers, though some of these structures have been hypothesized to result from the action of glaciers or lava.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kerr2005&#95;163-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kerr2005-163"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Jaeger2007&#95;164-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Jaeger2007-164"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> One of the larger examples, <a href="/wiki/Ma%27adim_Vallis" class="mw-redirect" title="Ma&#39;adim Vallis">Ma'adim Vallis</a>, is 700 kilometres (430&#160;mi) long, much greater than the Grand Canyon, with a width of 20 kilometres (12&#160;mi) and a depth of 2 kilometres (1.2&#160;mi) in places. It is thought to have been carved by flowing water early in Mars's history.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lucchita&#95;rosanova&#95;165-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lucchita_rosanova-165"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The youngest of these channels is thought to have formed only a few million years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nature434&#95;166-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nature434-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Elsewhere, particularly on the oldest areas of the Martian surface, finer-scale, dendritic <a href="/wiki/Valley_networks_(Mars)" class="mw-redirect" title="Valley networks (Mars)">networks of valleys</a> are spread across significant proportions of the landscape. Features of these valleys and their distribution strongly imply that they were carved by <a href="/wiki/Surface_runoff" title="Surface runoff">runoff</a> resulting from precipitation in early Mars history. Subsurface water flow and <a href="/wiki/Groundwater_sapping" title="Groundwater sapping">groundwater sapping</a> may play important subsidiary roles in some networks, but precipitation was probably the root cause of the incision in almost all cases.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CraddockHoward2002&#95;167-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CraddockHoward2002-167"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>165<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Along craters and canyon walls, there are thousands of features that appear similar to terrestrial <a href="/wiki/Gullies" class="mw-redirect" title="Gullies">gullies</a>. The gullies tend to be in the highlands of the Southern Hemisphere and face the Equator; all are poleward of 30° latitude. A number of authors have suggested that their formation process involves liquid water, probably from melting ice,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sci288&#95;168-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sci288-168"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>166<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa061206&#95;169-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa061206-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> although others have argued for formation mechanisms involving carbon dioxide frost or the movement of dry dust.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc061206&#95;170-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc061206-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa061206b&#95;171-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa061206b-171"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>169<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> No partially degraded gullies have formed by weathering and no superimposed impact craters have been observed, indicating that these are young features, possibly still active.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa061206&#95;169-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa061206-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Other geological features, such as <a href="/wiki/River_delta" title="River delta">deltas</a> and <a href="/wiki/Alluvial_fan" title="Alluvial fan">alluvial fans</a> preserved in craters, are further evidence for warmer, wetter conditions at an interval or intervals in earlier Mars history.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Lewis2006&#95;172-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lewis2006-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Such conditions necessarily require the widespread presence of <a href="/wiki/Volcanic_crater_lake" title="Volcanic crater lake">crater lakes</a> across a large proportion of the surface, for which there is independent mineralogical, sedimentological and geomorphological evidence.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Matsubara2011&#95;173-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Matsubara2011-173"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Further evidence that liquid water once existed on the surface of Mars comes from the detection of specific minerals such as <a href="/wiki/Hematite" title="Hematite">hematite</a> and <a href="/wiki/Goethite" title="Goethite">goethite</a>, both of which sometimes form in the presence of water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa040303&#95;174-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa040303-174"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="History_of_observations_and_findings_of_water_evidence">History of observations and findings of water evidence</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Water_on_Mars" title="Water on Mars">Water on Mars</a> and <a href="/wiki/Chronology_of_discoveries_of_water_on_Mars" title="Chronology of discoveries of water on Mars">Chronology of discoveries of water on Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Perspective_view_of_Korolev_crater.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Perspective_view_of_Korolev_crater.jpg/250px-Perspective_view_of_Korolev_crater.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Perspective_view_of_Korolev_crater.jpg/500px-Perspective_view_of_Korolev_crater.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1920" data-file-height="1080" /></a><figcaption>As top surface layer water appears readily visible at some places on Mars, as in this polar <a href="/wiki/Korolev_(Martian_crater)" title="Korolev (Martian crater)">crater called Korolev</a>, seen here in <a href="/wiki/3D_projection" title="3D projection">3D projection</a>.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The chemical signature of water vapor on Mars was first unequivocally demonstrated in 1963 by spectroscopy using an Earth-based telescope.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-175" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-176" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2004, <i>Opportunity</i> detected the mineral <a href="/wiki/Jarosite" title="Jarosite">jarosite</a>. This forms only in the presence of acidic water, showing that water once existed on Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa101001&#95;177-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa101001-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-178" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-178"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <i>Spirit</i> rover found concentrated deposits of <a href="/wiki/Silica" class="mw-redirect" title="Silica">silica</a> in 2007 that indicated wet conditions in the past, and in December 2011, the mineral <a href="/wiki/Gypsum" title="Gypsum">gypsum</a>, which also forms in the presence of water, was found on the surface by NASA's Mars rover Opportunity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-179" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa&#95;180-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-180"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nationalgeographic&#95;181-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nationalgeographic-181"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is estimated that the amount of water in the upper mantle of Mars, represented by <a href="/wiki/Hydroxyl_ion" class="mw-redirect" title="Hydroxyl ion">hydroxyl ions</a> contained within Martian minerals, is equal to or greater than that of Earth at 50–300 parts per million of water, which is enough to cover the entire planet to a depth of 200–1,000 metres (660–3,280&#160;ft).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nationalgeographic1&#95;182-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nationalgeographic1-182"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-183" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-183"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>181<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>On 18 March 2013, <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a> reported evidence from instruments on the <a href="/wiki/Curiosity_(rover)" title="Curiosity (rover)"><i>Curiosity</i> rover</a> of <a href="/wiki/Mineral_hydration" title="Mineral hydration">mineral hydration</a>, likely hydrated <a href="/wiki/Calcium_sulfate" title="Calcium sulfate">calcium sulfate</a>, in several <a href="/wiki/Rock_(geology)" title="Rock (geology)">rock samples</a> including the broken fragments of <a href="/wiki/Tintina_(rock)" title="Tintina (rock)">"Tintina" rock</a> and <a href="/wiki/List_of_rocks_on_Mars#Curiosity" title="List of rocks on Mars">"Sutton Inlier" rock</a> as well as in <a href="/wiki/Vein_(geology)" title="Vein (geology)">veins</a> and <a href="/wiki/Nodule_(geology)" title="Nodule (geology)">nodules</a> in other rocks like <a href="/wiki/List_of_rocks_on_Mars#Curiosity" title="List of rocks on Mars">"Knorr" rock</a> and <a href="/wiki/List_of_rocks_on_Mars#Curiosity" title="List of rocks on Mars">"Wernicke" rock</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA-20130318&#95;184-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-20130318-184"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BBC-20130319&#95;185-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BBC-20130319-185"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>183<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Analysis using the rover's <a href="/wiki/Curiosity_(rover)#Dynamic_Albedo_of_Neutrons_(DAN)" title="Curiosity (rover)">DAN instrument</a> provided evidence of subsurface water, amounting to as much as 4% water content, down to a depth of 60 centimetres (24&#160;in), during the rover's traverse from the <i><a href="/wiki/Bradbury_Landing" title="Bradbury Landing">Bradbury Landing</a></i> site to the <i>Yellowknife Bay</i> area in the <i><a href="/wiki/Glenelg,_Mars" title="Glenelg, Mars">Glenelg</a></i> terrain.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA-20130318&#95;184-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-20130318-184"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In September 2015, NASA announced that they had found strong evidence of hydrated <a href="/wiki/Brine" title="Brine">brine</a> flows in <a href="/wiki/Recurring_slope_lineae" class="mw-redirect" title="Recurring slope lineae">recurring slope lineae</a>, based on spectrometer readings of the darkened areas of slopes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-186" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-186"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>184<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-187" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-187"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>185<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ojhaetal2015&#95;188-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ojhaetal2015-188"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>186<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These streaks flow downhill in Martian summer, when the temperature is above −23&#160;°C, and freeze at lower temperatures.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-189" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-189"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These observations supported earlier hypotheses, based on timing of formation and their rate of growth, that these dark streaks resulted from water flowing just below the surface.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SeasonalFlows&#95;190-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SeasonalFlows-190"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>188<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, later work suggested that the lineae may be dry, granular flows instead, with at most a limited role for water in initiating the process.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-191" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-191"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>189<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A definitive conclusion about the presence, extent, and role of liquid water on the Martian surface remains elusive.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-192" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-192"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>190<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-193" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-193"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Researchers suspect much of the low northern plains of the planet were <a href="/wiki/Mars_ocean_hypothesis" title="Mars ocean hypothesis">covered with an ocean</a> hundreds of meters deep, though this theory remains controversial.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Head1999&#95;194-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Head1999-194"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>192<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In March 2015, scientists stated that such an ocean might have been the size of Earth's <a href="/wiki/Arctic_Ocean" title="Arctic Ocean">Arctic Ocean</a>. This finding was derived from the ratio of <a href="/wiki/Protium_(isotope)" class="mw-redirect" title="Protium (isotope)">protium</a> to <a href="/wiki/Deuterium" title="Deuterium">deuterium</a> in the modern Martian atmosphere compared to that ratio on Earth. The amount of Martian deuterium (D/H = 9.3 ± 1.7 10<sup>−4</sup>) is five to seven times the amount on Earth (D/H = 1.56 10<sup>−4</sup>), suggesting that ancient Mars had significantly higher levels of water. Results from the <i>Curiosity</i> rover had previously found a high ratio of deuterium in <a href="/wiki/Gale_Crater" class="mw-redirect" title="Gale Crater">Gale Crater</a>, though not significantly high enough to suggest the former presence of an ocean. Other scientists caution that these results have not been confirmed, and point out that Martian climate models have not yet shown that the planet was warm enough in the past to support bodies of liquid water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYT-20150305&#95;195-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT-20150305-195"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>193<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Near the northern polar cap is the 81.4 kilometres (50.6&#160;mi) wide <a href="/wiki/Korolev_(Martian_crater)" title="Korolev (Martian crater)">Korolev Crater</a>, which the <a href="/wiki/Mars_Express" title="Mars Express">Mars Express</a> orbiter found to be filled with approximately 2,200 cubic kilometres (530&#160;cu&#160;mi) of water ice.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-TG1218&#95;196-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-TG1218-196"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In November 2016, NASA reported finding a large amount of underground ice in the <a href="/wiki/Utopia_Planitia" title="Utopia Planitia">Utopia Planitia</a> region. The volume of water detected has been estimated to be equivalent to the volume of water in <a href="/wiki/Lake_Superior" title="Lake Superior">Lake Superior</a> (which is 12,100 cubic kilometers<sup id="cite&#95;ref-197" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-198" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-198"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA-20161122&#95;199-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-20161122-199"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> During observations from 2018 through 2021, the ExoMars Trace Gas Orbiter spotted indications of water, probably subsurface ice, in the Valles Marineris canyon system.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-200" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-200"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>198<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Orbital_motion">Orbital motion</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Orbit_of_Mars" title="Orbit of Mars">Orbit of Mars</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Timekeeping_on_Mars" title="Timekeeping on Mars">Timekeeping on Mars</a></div><p><span class="anchor" id="Viewing&#95;from&#95;Earth"></span><span class="anchor" id="Orbit&#95;and&#95;rotation"></span></p><figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Solar_system_orrery_inner_planets.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Mars circling the Sun further and slower than Earth" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Solar_system_orrery_inner_planets.gif/330px-Solar_system_orrery_inner_planets.gif" decoding="async" width="300" height="225" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/cc/Solar_system_orrery_inner_planets.gif 2x" data-file-width="800" data-file-height="600" /></a><figcaption>Orbit of Mars and other Inner Solar System planets</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mars's average distance from the Sun is roughly 230&#160;million&#160;km (143&#160;million&#160;mi), and its orbital period is 687 (Earth) days. The solar day (or <a href="/wiki/Mars_sol" title="Mars sol">sol</a>) on Mars is only slightly longer than an Earth day: 24 hours, 39 minutes, and 35.244 seconds.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-201" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-201"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>199<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A Martian year is equal to 1.8809 Earth years, or 1 year, 320 days, and 18.2 hours.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NasaFactSheet&#95;3-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NasaFactSheet-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_potential" title="Gravitational potential">gravitational potential</a> difference and thus the <a href="/wiki/Delta-v_budget" title="Delta-v budget">delta-v needed</a> to transfer between Mars and Earth is the second lowest for Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Petropoulos&#95;Longuski&#95;Bonfiglio&#95;2000&#95;pp.&#95;776–783&#95;202-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Petropoulos_Longuski_Bonfiglio_2000_pp._776–783-202"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>200<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Taylor&#95;2020&#95;203-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Taylor_2020-203"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>201<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The axial tilt of Mars is 25.19° relative to its <a href="/wiki/Orbital_plane" title="Orbital plane">orbital plane</a>, which is similar to the <a href="/wiki/Ecliptic#Obliquity_of_the_ecliptic" title="Ecliptic">axial tilt of Earth</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NasaFactSheet&#95;3-15" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NasaFactSheet-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As a result, Mars has seasons like Earth, though on Mars they are nearly twice as long because its orbital period is that much longer. In the present day, the orientation of the <a href="/wiki/North_pole" class="mw-redirect" title="North pole">north pole</a> of Mars is close to the star <a href="/wiki/Deneb" title="Deneb">Deneb</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-barlow08&#95;23-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-barlow08-23"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Mars has a relatively pronounced <a href="/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity" title="Orbital eccentricity">orbital eccentricity</a> of about 0.09; of the seven other planets in the Solar System, only <a href="/wiki/Mercury_(planet)" title="Mercury (planet)">Mercury</a> has a larger orbital eccentricity. It is known that in the past, Mars has had a much more circular orbit. At one point, 1.35&#160;million Earth years ago, Mars had an eccentricity of roughly 0.002, much less than that of Earth today.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mars&#95;eccentricity&#95;204-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mars_eccentricity-204"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>202<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mars's <a href="/wiki/Milankovitch_cycles" title="Milankovitch cycles">cycle of eccentricity</a> is 96,000 Earth years compared to Earth's cycle of 100,000 years.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Meeus2003&#95;205-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Meeus2003-205"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Mars has its closest approach to Earth (<a href="/wiki/Opposition_(astronomy)" title="Opposition (astronomy)">opposition</a>) in a <a href="/wiki/Synodic_period" class="mw-redirect" title="Synodic period">synodic period</a> of 779.94 days. It should not be confused with Mars <a href="/wiki/Conjunction_(astronomy)" title="Conjunction (astronomy)">conjunction</a>, where the Earth and Mars are at opposite sides of the Solar System and form a straight line crossing the Sun. The average time between the successive oppositions of Mars, its <a href="/wiki/Synodic_period" class="mw-redirect" title="Synodic period">synodic period</a>, is 780 days; but the number of days between successive oppositions can range from 764 to 812.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Meeus2003&#95;205-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Meeus2003-205"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The distance at close approach varies between about 54 and 103&#160;million&#160;km (34 and 64&#160;million&#160;mi) due to the planets' <a href="/wiki/Ellipse" title="Ellipse">elliptical</a> orbits, which causes comparable variation in <a href="/wiki/Angular_size" class="mw-redirect" title="Angular size">angular size</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Laskar2003&#95;206-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Laskar2003-206"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At their furthest Mars and Earth can be as far as 401&#160;million&#160;km (249&#160;million&#160;mi) apart.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-m455&#95;207-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-m455-207"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mars comes into opposition from Earth every 2.1 years. The planets come into opposition near Mars's <a href="/wiki/Perihelion" class="mw-redirect" title="Perihelion">perihelion</a> in 2003, 2018 and 2035, with the 2020 and 2033 events being particularly close to perihelic opposition.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:1&#95;208-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-209" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-209"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>207<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-210" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_-_2020_Opposition_(crop).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Mars_-_2020_Opposition_%28crop%29.jpg/250px-Mars_-_2020_Opposition_%28crop%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Mars_-_2020_Opposition_%28crop%29.jpg/500px-Mars_-_2020_Opposition_%28crop%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="960" data-file-height="960" /></a><figcaption>Mars seen through a 16-inch amateur telescope, at 2020 opposition</figcaption></figure>
<p>The mean <a href="/wiki/Apparent_magnitude" title="Apparent magnitude">apparent magnitude</a> of Mars is +0.71 with a standard deviation of 1.05.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Mallama&#95;and&#95;Hilton&#95;21-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Mallama_and_Hilton-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Because the orbit of Mars is eccentric, the magnitude at <a href="/wiki/Opposition_(planets)" class="mw-redirect" title="Opposition (planets)">opposition</a> from the Sun can range from about −3.0 to −1.4.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MallamaSky&#95;211-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MallamaSky-211"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>209<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The minimum brightness is magnitude +1.86 when the planet is near <a href="/wiki/Apsis" title="Apsis">aphelion</a> and in <a href="/wiki/Conjunction_(astronomy)" title="Conjunction (astronomy)">conjunction</a> with the Sun.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Mallama&#95;and&#95;Hilton&#95;21-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Mallama_and_Hilton-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At its brightest, Mars (along with <a href="/wiki/Jupiter" title="Jupiter">Jupiter</a>) is second only to Venus in apparent brightness.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Mallama&#95;and&#95;Hilton&#95;21-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Mallama_and_Hilton-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mars usually appears distinctly yellow, orange, or red. When farthest away from Earth, it is more than seven times farther away than when it is closest. Mars is usually close enough for particularly good viewing once or twice at 15-year or 17-year intervals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-212" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-212"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>210<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Optical ground-based telescopes are typically limited to resolving features about 300 kilometres (190&#160;mi) across when Earth and Mars are closest because of Earth's atmosphere.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-usra&#95;213-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-usra-213"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>211<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>As Mars approaches opposition, it begins a period of <a href="/wiki/Apparent_retrograde_motion" title="Apparent retrograde motion">retrograde motion</a>, which means it will appear to move backwards in a looping curve with respect to the background stars. This retrograde motion lasts for about 72 days, and Mars reaches its peak apparent brightness in the middle of this interval.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zeilik02&#95;214-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zeilik02-214"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>212<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Moons">Moons</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Moons_of_Mars" title="Moons of Mars">Moons of Mars</a>, <a href="/wiki/Phobos_(moon)" title="Phobos (moon)">Phobos (moon)</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Deimos_(moon)" title="Deimos (moon)">Deimos (moon)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:PIA17351-ApparentSizes-MarsDeimosPhobos-EarthMoon.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/PIA17351-ApparentSizes-MarsDeimosPhobos-EarthMoon.jpg/250px-PIA17351-ApparentSizes-MarsDeimosPhobos-EarthMoon.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/0c/PIA17351-ApparentSizes-MarsDeimosPhobos-EarthMoon.jpg 2x" data-file-width="300" data-file-height="300" /></a><figcaption>The moons of Mars <a href="/wiki/Deimos_(moon)" title="Deimos (moon)">Deimos</a> and <a href="/wiki/Phobos_(moon)" title="Phobos (moon)">Phobos</a> as seen from Mars, compared at <a href="/wiki/Angular_size" class="mw-redirect" title="Angular size">apparent size</a> to the in apparent and actual size much larger <a href="/wiki/Moon" title="Moon">Moon</a> as seen from Earth. If they would be as far away from Mars as the Moon from Earth, they would appear as faint star-like features in the Martian sky.</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Phobos_fly-by_animation_ESA223006.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Phobos_fly-by_animation_ESA223006.gif/250px-Phobos_fly-by_animation_ESA223006.gif" decoding="async" width="250" height="128" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Phobos_fly-by_animation_ESA223006.gif/500px-Phobos_fly-by_animation_ESA223006.gif 2x" data-file-width="577" data-file-height="295" /></a><figcaption>Orbits of natural and artificial satellites around Mars at scale, with the furthest (Deimos) at 23,460&#160;km (14,580&#160;mi)</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mars has two relatively small (compared to Earth's) natural moons, <a href="/wiki/Phobos_(moon)" title="Phobos (moon)">Phobos</a> (about 22&#160;km (14&#160;mi) in diameter) and <a href="/wiki/Deimos_(moon)" title="Deimos (moon)">Deimos</a> (about 12&#160;km (7.5&#160;mi) in diameter), which orbit at 9,376&#160;km (5,826&#160;mi) and 23,460&#160;km (14,580&#160;mi) around the planet. The origin of both moons is unclear, although a popular theory states that they were asteroids captured into Martian orbit.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa31031&#95;215-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa31031-215"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>213<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Both satellites were discovered in 1877 by <a href="/wiki/Asaph_Hall" title="Asaph Hall">Asaph Hall</a> and were named after the characters <a href="/wiki/Phobos_(mythology)" title="Phobos (mythology)">Phobos</a> (the deity of panic and fear) and <a href="/wiki/Deimos_(mythology)" class="mw-redirect" title="Deimos (mythology)">Deimos</a> (the deity of terror and dread), twins from <a href="/wiki/Greek_mythology" title="Greek mythology">Greek mythology</a> who accompanied their father <a href="/wiki/Ares" title="Ares">Ares</a>, god of war, into battle.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-216" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-216"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>214<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Mars_(mythology)" title="Mars (mythology)">Mars</a> was the Roman equivalent to Ares. In modern <a href="/wiki/Greek_language" title="Greek language">Greek</a>, the planet retains its ancient name <i>Ares</i> (Aris: <i>Άρης</i>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:6&#95;104-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:6-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>From the surface of Mars, the motions of Phobos and Deimos appear different from that of the Earth's satellite, the <a href="/wiki/Moon" title="Moon">Moon</a>. Phobos rises in the west, sets in the east, and rises again in just 11 hours. Deimos, being only just outside <a href="/wiki/Synchronous_orbit" title="Synchronous orbit">synchronous orbit</a>&#160;&#8211;&#32;where the orbital period would match the planet's period of rotation&#160;&#8211;&#32;rises as expected in the east, but slowly. Because the orbit of Phobos is below a synchronous altitude, <a href="/wiki/Tidal_force" title="Tidal force">tidal forces</a> from Mars are gradually lowering its orbit. In about 50&#160;million years, it could either crash into Mars's surface or break up into a ring structure around the planet.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-217" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-217"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>215<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The origin of the two satellites is not well understood. Their low albedo and <a href="/wiki/Carbonaceous_chondrite" title="Carbonaceous chondrite">carbonaceous chondrite</a> composition have been regarded as similar to asteroids, supporting a capture theory. The unstable orbit of Phobos would seem to point toward a relatively recent capture. But both have <a href="/wiki/Circular_orbit" title="Circular orbit">circular orbits</a> near the equator, which is unusual for captured objects, and the required capture dynamics are complex. Accretion early in the history of Mars is plausible, but would not account for a composition resembling asteroids rather than Mars itself, if that is confirmed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-218" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-218"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>216<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mars may have yet-undiscovered moons, smaller than 50 to 100 metres (160 to 330&#160;ft) in diameter, and a dust ring is predicted to exist between Phobos and Deimos.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-adler&#95;219-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-adler-219"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>217<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A third possibility for their origin as satellites of Mars is the involvement of a third body or a type of impact disruption. More-recent lines of evidence for Phobos having a highly porous interior,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Andert&#95;220-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Andert-220"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>218<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and suggesting a composition containing mainly <a href="/wiki/Phyllosilicates" class="mw-redirect" title="Phyllosilicates">phyllosilicates</a> and other minerals known from Mars,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Giuranna&#95;221-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Giuranna-221"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> point toward an origin of Phobos from material ejected by an impact on Mars that reaccreted in Martian orbit, similar to the <a href="/wiki/Giant_impact_hypothesis" class="mw-redirect" title="Giant impact hypothesis">prevailing theory</a> for the origin of Earth's satellite. Although the <a href="/wiki/Visible_and_near-infrared" class="mw-redirect" title="Visible and near-infrared">visible and near-infrared</a> (VNIR) spectra of the moons of Mars resemble those of outer-belt asteroids, the <a href="/wiki/Thermal_infrared" class="mw-redirect" title="Thermal infrared">thermal infrared</a> spectra of Phobos are reported to be inconsistent with <a href="/wiki/Chondrite" title="Chondrite">chondrites</a> of any class.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Giuranna&#95;221-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Giuranna-221"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is also possible that Phobos and Deimos were fragments of an older moon, formed by debris from a large impact on Mars, and then destroyed by a more recent impact upon the satellite.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-222" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-222"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>220<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>More recently, a study conducted by a team of researchers from multiple countries suggests that a lost moon, at least fifteen times the size of Phobos, may have existed in the past. By analyzing rocks which point to tidal processes on the planet, it is possible that these tides may have been regulated by a past moon.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-223" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-223"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>221<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Human_observations_and_exploration">Human observations and exploration</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Exploration_of_Mars" title="Exploration of Mars">Exploration of Mars</a> and <a href="/wiki/History_of_Mars_observation" title="History of Mars observation">History of Mars observation</a></div>
<p>The history of observations of Mars is marked by oppositions of Mars when the planet is closest to Earth and hence is most easily visible, which occur every couple of years. Even more notable are the <a href="/wiki/Perihelic_opposition" class="mw-redirect" title="Perihelic opposition">perihelic oppositions</a> of Mars, which are distinguished because Mars is close to perihelion, making it even closer to Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:1&#95;208-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Ancient_observations">Ancient observations</h3></div>
<p>The ancient <a href="/wiki/Sumer" title="Sumer">Sumerians</a> named Mars <a href="/wiki/Nergal" title="Nergal">Nergal</a>, the god of war and plague. During Sumerian times, Nergal was a minor deity of little significance, but, during later times, his main cult center was the city of <a href="/wiki/Nineveh" title="Nineveh">Nineveh</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Rabkin2005&#95;224-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Rabkin2005-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In Mesopotamian texts, Mars is referred to as the "star of judgement of the fate of the dead".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-225" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-225"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>223<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The existence of Mars as a wandering object in the night sky was also recorded by the ancient <a href="/wiki/Egyptian_astronomy" title="Egyptian astronomy">Egyptian astronomers</a> and, by 1534 BCE, they were familiar with the <a href="/wiki/Apparent_retrograde_motion" title="Apparent retrograde motion">retrograde motion</a> of the planet.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-paob85&#95;226-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-paob85-226"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By the period of the <a href="/wiki/Neo-Babylonian_Empire" title="Neo-Babylonian Empire">Neo-Babylonian Empire</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy" title="Babylonian astronomy">Babylonian astronomers</a> were making regular records of the positions of the planets and systematic observations of their behavior. For Mars, they knew that the planet made 37 <a href="/wiki/Orbital_period" title="Orbital period">synodic periods</a>, or 42 circuits of the zodiac, every 79 years. They invented arithmetic methods for making minor corrections to the predicted positions of the planets.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-north08&#95;227-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-north08-227"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>225<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-swerdlow98&#95;228-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-swerdlow98-228"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Greece" title="Ancient Greece">Ancient Greece</a>, the planet was known as <span title="Ancient Greek-language text"><span lang="grc"><a href="/wiki/Pyroeis" title="Pyroeis">Πυρόεις</a></span></span>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-229" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-229"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Commonly, the Greek name for the planet now referred to as Mars, was Ares. It was the Romans who named the planet Mars, for their god of war, often represented by the sword and shield of the planet's namesake.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-230" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-230"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>228<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the fourth century BCE, <a href="/wiki/Aristotle" title="Aristotle">Aristotle</a> noted that Mars disappeared behind the Moon during an <a href="/wiki/Occultation" title="Occultation">occultation</a>, indicating that the planet was farther away.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-231" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-231"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>229<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Ptolemy" title="Ptolemy">Ptolemy</a>, a Greek living in <a href="/wiki/Alexandria" title="Alexandria">Alexandria</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-harland&#95;232-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-harland-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> attempted to address the problem of the orbital motion of Mars. Ptolemy's model and his collective work on astronomy was presented in the multi-volume collection later called the <i><a href="/wiki/Almagest" title="Almagest">Almagest</a></i> (from the Arabic for "greatest"), which became the authoritative treatise on <a href="/wiki/Medieval_astronomy" class="mw-redirect" title="Medieval astronomy">Western astronomy</a> for the next fourteen centuries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-233" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-233"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Literature from ancient China confirms that Mars was known by <a href="/wiki/Chinese_astronomy" title="Chinese astronomy">Chinese astronomers</a> by no later than the fourth century BCE.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-needham&#95;ronan85&#95;234-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-needham_ronan85-234"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the <a href="/wiki/East_Asian" class="mw-redirect" title="East Asian">East Asian</a> cultures, Mars is traditionally referred to as the "fire star" (<span title="Chinese-language text"><span lang="zh">火星</span></span>) based on the <a href="/wiki/Wuxing_(Chinese_philosophy)" title="Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)"><i>Wuxing</i> system</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-235" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-236" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-237" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-237"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>235<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Early_modern_observations">Early modern observations</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Kepler_Mars_retrograde.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Kepler_Mars_retrograde.jpg/250px-Kepler_Mars_retrograde.jpg" decoding="async" width="230" height="209" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Kepler_Mars_retrograde.jpg/500px-Kepler_Mars_retrograde.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1060" data-file-height="964" /></a><figcaption>Diagram of the <a href="/wiki/Geocentric" class="mw-redirect" title="Geocentric">geocentric</a> trajectory of Mars through several periods of <a href="/wiki/Apparent_retrograde_motion" title="Apparent retrograde motion">apparent retrograde motion</a> in <a href="/wiki/Johannes_Kepler" title="Johannes Kepler">Johannes Kepler</a>'s <i>Astronomia Nova</i> (1609)</figcaption></figure>
<p>In 1609 <a href="/wiki/Johannes_Kepler" title="Johannes Kepler">Johannes Kepler</a> published a 10-year study of Martian orbit,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-v529&#95;238-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-v529-238"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>236<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> using the <a href="/wiki/Diurnal_parallax" class="mw-redirect" title="Diurnal parallax">diurnal parallax</a> of Mars, measured by <a href="/wiki/Tycho_Brahe" title="Tycho Brahe">Tycho Brahe</a>, to make a preliminary calculation of the relative distance to the planet.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-taton03&#95;239-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-taton03-239"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> From Brahe's observations of Mars, Kepler deduced that the planet orbited the Sun not in a circle, but in an <a href="/wiki/Ellipse" title="Ellipse">ellipse</a>. Moreover, Kepler showed that Mars sped up as it approached the Sun and slowed down as it moved farther away, in a manner that later physicists would explain as a consequence of the <a href="/wiki/Conservation_of_angular_momentum" class="mw-redirect" title="Conservation of angular momentum">conservation of angular momentum</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-TMU&#95;240-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-TMU-240"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Pages: 433–437">&#58;&#8202;433–437&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Lowell_-_Mars_(1894)_-_Huyghens%27_drawing_of_the_Syrtis_Major,_Nov._28,_1659.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Lowell_-_Mars_%281894%29_-_Huyghens%27_drawing_of_the_Syrtis_Major%2C_Nov._28%2C_1659.jpg/250px-Lowell_-_Mars_%281894%29_-_Huyghens%27_drawing_of_the_Syrtis_Major%2C_Nov._28%2C_1659.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="283" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Lowell_-_Mars_%281894%29_-_Huyghens%27_drawing_of_the_Syrtis_Major%2C_Nov._28%2C_1659.jpg/500px-Lowell_-_Mars_%281894%29_-_Huyghens%27_drawing_of_the_Syrtis_Major%2C_Nov._28%2C_1659.jpg 2x" data-file-width="610" data-file-height="690" /></a><figcaption>First drawn record of Martian features (Manuscript K), by <a href="/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens" title="Christiaan Huygens">Christiaan Huygens</a>, observed on 28 November 1659.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-v462&#95;241-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-v462-241"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>239<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The feature is probably <a href="/wiki/Syrtis_Major_Planum" title="Syrtis Major Planum">Syrtis Major Planum</a>. Huygens calculated from the moving of the features the rotation of Mars.</figcaption></figure>
<p>In 1610 the first use of a telescope for astronomical observation, including Mars, was performed by Italian astronomer <a href="/wiki/Galileo_Galilei" title="Galileo Galilei">Galileo Galilei</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jha15&#95;242-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jha15-242"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>240<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> With the telescope the diurnal parallax of Mars was again measured in an effort to determine the Sun-Earth distance. This was first performed by <a href="/wiki/Giovanni_Domenico_Cassini" title="Giovanni Domenico Cassini">Giovanni Domenico Cassini</a> in 1672. The early parallax measurements were hampered by the quality of the instruments.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hirschfeld01&#95;243-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hirschfeld01-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The only <a href="/wiki/Occultation" title="Occultation">occultation</a> of Mars by Venus observed was that of 13 October 1590, seen by <a href="/wiki/Michael_Maestlin" title="Michael Maestlin">Michael Maestlin</a> at <a href="/wiki/Heidelberg" title="Heidelberg">Heidelberg</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sat57&#95;244-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sat57-244"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>242<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Martian_&quot;canals&quot;"><span id="Martian_.22canals.22"></span>Martian "canals"</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Martian_canals" title="Martian canals">Martian canals</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_-_MEC-1_Prototype._LOC_2013593160.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Mars_-_MEC-1_Prototype._LOC_2013593160.jpg/500px-Mars_-_MEC-1_Prototype._LOC_2013593160.jpg" decoding="async" width="380" height="287" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Mars_-_MEC-1_Prototype._LOC_2013593160.jpg/960px-Mars_-_MEC-1_Prototype._LOC_2013593160.jpg 2x" data-file-width="11623" data-file-height="8787" /></a><figcaption>A 1962 map of Mars published by the U.S. <a href="/wiki/Aeronautical_Chart_and_Information_Center" class="mw-redirect" title="Aeronautical Chart and Information Center">Aeronautical Chart and Information Center</a>, showing canals snaking through the Martian landscape. At the time, the existence of canals was still highly controversial as no close-up pictures of Mars had been taken (until <a href="/wiki/Mariner_4" title="Mariner 4">Mariner 4</a>'s flyby in 1965).</figcaption></figure>
<p>By the 19th century, the resolution of telescopes reached a level sufficient for surface features to be identified. On 5 September 1877, a perihelic opposition to Mars occurred. The Italian astronomer <a href="/wiki/Giovanni_Schiaparelli" title="Giovanni Schiaparelli">Giovanni Schiaparelli</a> used a 22-centimetre (8.7&#160;in) telescope in <a href="/wiki/Milan" title="Milan">Milan</a> to help produce the first detailed map of Mars.
