These maps notably contained features he called <i>canali</i>, which, with the possible exception of the natural canyon <a href="/wiki/Valles_Marineris" title="Valles Marineris">Valles Marineris</a>, were later shown to be an <a href="/wiki/Optical_illusion" title="Optical illusion">optical illusion</a>. These <i>canali</i> were supposedly long, straight lines on the surface of Mars, to which he gave names of famous rivers on Earth. His term, which means "channels" or "grooves", was popularly mistranslated in English as "canals".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-245" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-245"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sagan80&#95;246-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sagan80-246"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Influenced by the observations, the orientalist <a href="/wiki/Percival_Lowell" title="Percival Lowell">Percival Lowell</a> founded <a href="/wiki/Lowell_Observatory" title="Lowell Observatory">an observatory</a> which had 30- and 45-centimetre (12- and 18-in) telescopes. The observatory was used for the exploration of Mars during the last good opportunity in 1894, and the following less favorable oppositions. He published several books on Mars and life on the planet, which had a great influence on the public.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-basalla06&#95;247-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-basalla06-247"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>245<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYT-20151001&#95;248-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT-20151001-248"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>246<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <i>canali</i> were independently observed by other astronomers, like <a href="/wiki/Henri_Joseph_Perrotin" class="mw-redirect" title="Henri Joseph Perrotin">Henri Joseph Perrotin</a> and <a href="/wiki/Louis_Thollon" title="Louis Thollon">Louis Thollon</a> in Nice, using one of the largest telescopes of that time.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-maria&#95;lane05&#95;249-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-maria_lane05-249"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ba3&#95;250-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ba3-250"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The seasonal changes (consisting of the diminishing of the polar caps and the dark areas formed during Martian summers) in combination with the canals led to speculation about life on Mars, and it was a long-held belief that Mars contained vast seas and vegetation. As bigger telescopes were used, fewer long, straight <i>canali</i> were observed. During observations in 1909 by <a href="/wiki/Antoniadi" class="mw-redirect" title="Antoniadi">Antoniadi</a> with an 84-centimetre (33&#160;in) telescope, irregular patterns were observed, but no <i>canali</i> were seen.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zahnle01&#95;251-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zahnle01-251"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="First_exploration">First exploration</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:M04_12.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/M04_12.jpg/250px-M04_12.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/64/M04_12.jpg 2x" data-file-width="400" data-file-height="400" /></a><figcaption>The very first two images from, and of, another planet (Mars), by <a href="/wiki/Mariner_4" title="Mariner 4">Mariner 4</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-u445&#95;252-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-u445-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>250<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the second spacecraft to reach Mars and the first to transmit data (15 July 1965). The data disproved Mars being hospitable and having canals.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The first spacecraft from Earth to visit Mars was <a href="/wiki/Mars_1" title="Mars 1">Mars 1</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Soviet_space_program" title="Soviet space program">Soviet Union</a>, which flew by in 1963, but contact was lost en route. <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a>'s <i><a href="/wiki/Mariner_4" title="Mariner 4">Mariner 4</a></i> followed and became the first spacecraft to successfully transmit from Mars; launched on 28 November 1964, it made its closest approach to the planet on 15 July 1965. <i>Mariner 4</i> detected the weak Martian radiation belt, measured at about 0.1% that of Earth, and captured the first images of another planet from deep space.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-253" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-253"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Once <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft" title="Spacecraft">spacecraft</a> visited the planet during the 1960s and 1970s, many previous concepts of Mars were radically broken. After the results of the <i>Viking</i> life-detection experiments, the hypothesis of a dead planet was generally accepted.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ward&#95;brownlee00&#95;254-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ward_brownlee00-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>252<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The data from <i>Mariner 9</i> and <i>Viking</i> allowed better maps of Mars to be made.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Renewed_exploration">Renewed exploration</h3></div>
<p>Until 1997 and after Viking 1 shut down in 1982, Mars was only visited by three unsuccessful probes, two flying past without contact (<a href="/wiki/Phobos_1" title="Phobos 1">Phobos 1</a>, 1988; <a href="/wiki/Mars_Observer" title="Mars Observer">Mars Observer</a>, 1993), and one (<a href="/wiki/Phobos_2" title="Phobos 2">Phobos 2</a> 1989) malfunctioning in orbit before reaching its destination <a href="/wiki/Phobos_(moon)" title="Phobos (moon)">Phobos</a>.
</p><p>In 1997 <a href="/wiki/Mars_Pathfinder" title="Mars Pathfinder">Mars Pathfinder</a> became the first successful rover mission beyond the Moon and started together with <i><a href="/wiki/Mars_Global_Surveyor" title="Mars Global Surveyor">Mars Global Surveyor</a></i> (operated until late 2006) an uninterrupted active robotic presence at Mars that has lasted until today. It produced complete, extremely detailed maps of the Martian topography, magnetic field and surface minerals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Distant&#95;worlds:&#95;milestones&#95;in&#95;planetary&#95;exploration&#95;255-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Distant_worlds:_milestones_in_planetary_exploration-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>253<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Starting with these missions a range of new improved crewless <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft" title="Spacecraft">spacecraft</a>, including <a href="/wiki/Orbiter" class="mw-redirect" title="Orbiter">orbiters</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mars_lander" class="mw-redirect" title="Mars lander">landers</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Mars_rover" title="Mars rover">rovers</a>, have been sent to Mars, with successful missions by the NASA (United States), <a href="/wiki/Jaxa" class="mw-redirect" title="Jaxa">Jaxa</a> (<a href="/wiki/Japan" title="Japan">Japan</a>), <a href="/wiki/ESA" class="mw-redirect" title="ESA">ESA</a>, <a href="/wiki/United_Kingdom" title="United Kingdom">United Kingdom</a>, <a href="/wiki/ISRO" title="ISRO">ISRO</a> (<a href="/wiki/India" title="India">India</a>), <a href="/wiki/Roscosmos" title="Roscosmos">Roscosmos</a> (Russia), the <a href="/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Space_Centre" title="Mohammed bin Rashid Space Centre">United Arab Emirates</a>, and <a href="/wiki/CNSA" class="mw-redirect" title="CNSA">CNSA</a> (<a href="/wiki/China" title="China">China</a>) to study the planet's surface, climate, and geology,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-256" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-256"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>254<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> uncovering the different elements of the history and dynamic of the hydrosphere of Mars and possible traces of ancient life.
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mars_2020_selfie_containing_both_perseverance_rover_and_ingenuity.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Mars_2020_selfie_containing_both_perseverance_rover_and_ingenuity.gif/250px-Mars_2020_selfie_containing_both_perseverance_rover_and_ingenuity.gif" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Mars_2020_selfie_containing_both_perseverance_rover_and_ingenuity.gif/500px-Mars_2020_selfie_containing_both_perseverance_rover_and_ingenuity.gif 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Self-portrait" title="Self-portrait">Self-portrait</a> of <i><a href="/wiki/Perseverance_(rover)" title="Perseverance (rover)">Perseverance</a></i> rover and <i><a href="/wiki/Ingenuity_(helicopter)" title="Ingenuity (helicopter)">Ingenuity</a></i> helicopter (left) at <a href="/wiki/Wright_Brothers_Field" class="mw-redirect" title="Wright Brothers Field">Wright Brothers Field</a>, 2021</figcaption></figure>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Current_missions">Current missions</h3></div>
<p>As of 2023<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mars&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>, Mars is host to ten functioning <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft" title="Spacecraft">spacecraft</a>.
</p><p>Eight are in orbit<i>: <a href="/wiki/2001_Mars_Odyssey" title="2001 Mars Odyssey">2001 Mars Odyssey</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Mars_Express" title="Mars Express">Mars Express</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Mars_Reconnaissance_Orbiter" title="Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter">Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/MAVEN" title="MAVEN">MAVEN</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/ExoMars_Trace_Gas_Orbiter" class="mw-redirect" title="ExoMars Trace Gas Orbiter">ExoMars Trace Gas Orbiter</a></i>, the <i><a href="/wiki/Emirates_Mars_Mission" title="Emirates Mars Mission">Hope</a></i> orbiter, and the <i><a href="/wiki/Tianwen-1" title="Tianwen-1">Tianwen-1</a></i> orbiter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-257" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-257"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>255<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-258" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-258"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>256<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Another two are on the surface: the <a href="/wiki/Mars_Science_Laboratory" title="Mars Science Laboratory">Mars Science Laboratory</a> <i><a href="/wiki/Curiosity_(rover)" title="Curiosity (rover)">Curiosity</a></i> rover and the <i><a href="/wiki/Perseverance_(rover)" title="Perseverance (rover)">Perseverance</a></i> rover.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nyt-china-2021&#95;259-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nyt-china-2021-259"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>257<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Collected maps are available online at websites including <a href="/wiki/Google_Mars" class="mw-redirect" title="Google Mars">Google Mars</a>. NASA provides two online tools: Mars Trek, which provides visualizations of the planet using data from 50 years of exploration, and <a href="/wiki/Experience_Curiosity" title="Experience Curiosity">Experience Curiosity</a>, which simulates traveling on Mars in 3-D with <i>Curiosity</i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-260" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-260"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>258<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-261" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-261"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>259<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Future">Future</h3></div>
<p>Planned missions to Mars include:
</p>
<ul><li>NASA's <a href="/wiki/EscaPADE" class="mw-redirect" title="EscaPADE">EscaPADE</a> spacecraft, launched on 13 November 2025.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa2024062024&#95;262-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa2024062024-262"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>260<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-263" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-263"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>261<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-264" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-264"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>262<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li>The <a href="/wiki/Rosalind_Franklin_(rover)" title="Rosalind Franklin (rover)"><i>Rosalind Franklin</i> rover</a> mission, designed to search for evidence of past life, which was intended to be launched in 2018 but has been repeatedly delayed, with a launch date pushed to 2028 at the earliest.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-265" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-265"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>263<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-266" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-266"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>264<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-267" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-267"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>265<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The project was restarted in 2024 with additional funding.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-268" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-268"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li>A current concept for a joint <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a>-<a href="/wiki/European_Space_Agency" title="European Space Agency">ESA</a> mission to <a href="/wiki/NASA-ESA_Mars_Sample_Return" title="NASA-ESA Mars Sample Return">return samples from Mars</a> would launch in 2026.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-269" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-269"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>267<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-270" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-270"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>268<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li>China's <a href="/wiki/Tianwen-3" title="Tianwen-3">Tianwen-3</a>, a sample return mission, scheduled to launch in either 2028 or 2030.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-271" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-271"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>269<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<p>As of February&#160;2024<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mars&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>, debris from these types of missions has reached over seven tons. Most of it consists of crashed and inactive spacecraft as well as discarded components.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-272" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-272"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-273" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-273"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>271<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In April 2024, NASA selected several companies to begin studies on providing commercial services to further enable robotic science on Mars. Key areas include establishing telecommunications, payload delivery and surface imaging.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-274" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-274"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>272<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Habitability_and_habitation">Habitability and habitation</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Life_on_Mars" title="Life on Mars">Life on Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:41586_2025_9413_Fig3_HTML.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/41586_2025_9413_Fig3_HTML.png/330px-41586_2025_9413_Fig3_HTML.png" decoding="async" width="300" height="228" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/41586_2025_9413_Fig3_HTML.png/960px-41586_2025_9413_Fig3_HTML.png 2x" data-file-width="1831" data-file-height="1390" /></a><figcaption>Layering, nodules, reaction fronts and organic detections marked in an image from ancient fossilized mud at <a href="/wiki/Cheyava_Falls" title="Cheyava Falls">Cheyava Falls</a>, being the most promising finds potentially showing traces of remains of ancient Martian life.</figcaption></figure>
<p>During the late 19th century, it was widely accepted in the astronomical community that Mars had life-supporting qualities, including the presence of oxygen and water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CampbellWW&#95;275-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CampbellWW-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, in 1894 <a href="/wiki/William_Wallace_Campbell" title="William Wallace Campbell">W. W. Campbell</a> at <a href="/wiki/Lick_Observatory" title="Lick Observatory">Lick Observatory</a> observed the planet and found that "if water vapor or oxygen occur in the atmosphere of Mars it is in quantities too small to be detected by spectroscopes then available".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CampbellWW&#95;275-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CampbellWW-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> That observation contradicted many of the measurements of the time and was not widely accepted.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CampbellWW&#95;275-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CampbellWW-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Campbell and <a href="/wiki/Vesto_Slipher" title="Vesto Slipher">V. M. Slipher</a> repeated the study in 1909 using better instruments, but with the same results. It was not until the findings were confirmed by <a href="/wiki/Walter_Sydney_Adams" title="Walter Sydney Adams">W. S. Adams</a> in 1925 that the myth of the Earth-like habitability of Mars was finally broken.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CampbellWW&#95;275-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CampbellWW-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, even in the 1960s, articles were published on Martian biology, putting aside explanations other than life for the seasonal changes on Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-science136&#95;3510&#95;276-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-science136_3510-276"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>274<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The current understanding of <a href="/wiki/Planetary_habitability" title="Planetary habitability">planetary habitability</a>&#160;&#8211;&#32;the ability of a world to develop environmental conditions favorable to the emergence of life&#160;&#8211;&#32;favors planets that have liquid water on their surface. Most often this requires the orbit of a planet to lie within the <a href="/wiki/Circumstellar_habitable_zone" class="mw-redirect" title="Circumstellar habitable zone">habitable zone</a>, which for the Sun is estimated to extend from within the orbit of Earth to about that of Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kopparapu2013b&#95;277-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kopparapu2013b-277"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>275<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> During perihelion, Mars dips inside this region, but Mars's thin (low-pressure) atmosphere prevents liquid water from existing over large regions for extended periods. The past flow of liquid water demonstrates the planet's potential for habitability. Recent evidence has suggested that any water on the Martian surface may have been too salty and acidic to support regular terrestrial life.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-saltlife&#95;278-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-saltlife-278"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>276<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The environmental conditions on Mars are a challenge to sustaining organic life: the planet has little <a href="/wiki/Heat_transfer" title="Heat transfer">heat transfer</a> across its surface, it has poor insulation against <a href="/wiki/Solar_wind#Atmospheres" title="Solar wind">bombardment</a> by the <a href="/wiki/Solar_wind" title="Solar wind">solar wind</a> due to the absence of a magnetosphere and has insufficient atmospheric pressure to retain water in a liquid form (water instead <a href="/wiki/Sublimation_(phase_transition)" title="Sublimation (phase transition)">sublimes</a> to a gaseous state). Mars is nearly, or perhaps totally, geologically dead; the end of volcanic activity has apparently stopped the recycling of chemicals and minerals between the surface and interior of the planet.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hannsson97&#95;279-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hannsson97-279"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>277<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Evidence suggests that the planet was once significantly more habitable than it is today, but whether living <a href="/wiki/Organism" title="Organism">organisms</a> ever existed there remains unknown. The <a href="/wiki/Viking_probes" class="mw-redirect" title="Viking probes"><i>Viking</i> probes</a> of the mid-1970s carried experiments designed to detect microorganisms in Martian soil at their respective landing sites and had positive results, including a temporary increase in <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1123817410" /><span class="chemf nowrap">CO<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span> production on exposure to water and nutrients. This sign of life was later disputed by scientists, resulting in a continuing debate, with NASA scientist <a href="/wiki/Gilbert_Levin" title="Gilbert Levin">Gilbert Levin</a> asserting that <i>Viking</i> may have found life.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nytobit&#95;280-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nytobit-280"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>278<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A 2014 analysis of Martian meteorite EETA79001 found <a href="/wiki/Chlorate" title="Chlorate">chlorate</a>, <a href="/wiki/Perchlorate" title="Perchlorate">perchlorate</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Nitrate" title="Nitrate">nitrate</a> ions in sufficiently high concentrations to suggest that they are widespread on Mars. UV and X-ray radiation would turn chlorate and perchlorate ions into other, highly <a href="/wiki/Redox" title="Redox">reactive</a> <a href="/wiki/Oxyanion" title="Oxyanion">oxychlorines</a>, indicating that any organic molecules would have to be buried under the surface to survive.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-281" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-281"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>279<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Small quantities of <a href="/wiki/Methane" title="Methane">methane</a> and <a href="/wiki/Formaldehyde" title="Formaldehyde">formaldehyde</a> detected by Mars orbiters are both claimed to be possible evidence for life, as these <a href="/wiki/Chemical_compound" title="Chemical compound">chemical compounds</a> would quickly break down in the Martian atmosphere.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-icarus172&#95;282-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-icarus172-282"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>280<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-form&#95;283-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-form-283"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>281<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Alternatively, these compounds may instead be replenished by volcanic or other geological means, such as <a href="/wiki/Serpentinite" title="Serpentinite">serpentinite</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-olivine&#95;139-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-olivine-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Impactite" title="Impactite">Impact glass</a>, formed by the impact of meteors, which on Earth can preserve signs of life, has also been found on the surface of the impact craters on Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-brown20140418&#95;284-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-brown20140418-284"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>282<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Schultz2014&#95;285-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Schultz2014-285"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>283<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Likewise, the glass in impact craters on Mars could have preserved signs of life, if life existed at the site.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasapr20150608&#95;286-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasapr20150608-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>284<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-brown20150608&#95;287-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-brown20150608-287"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>285<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sciam20150612&#95;288-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sciam20150612-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>286<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Cheyava_Falls" title="Cheyava Falls">Cheyava Falls</a> rock discovered on Mars in June 2024 has been designated by NASA as a "potential <a href="/wiki/Biosignature" title="Biosignature">biosignature</a>" and was core sampled by the <a href="/wiki/Perseverance_(rover)" title="Perseverance (rover)">Perseverance</a> rover for possible return to Earth and further examination. Although highly intriguing, no definitive final determination on a biological or abiotic origin of this rock can be made with the data currently available.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-289" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-289"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>287<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Human_mission_proposals">Human mission proposals</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:NextSTEP_mars-soil-excavation.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/NextSTEP_mars-soil-excavation.jpg/330px-NextSTEP_mars-soil-excavation.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="169" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/NextSTEP_mars-soil-excavation.jpg/960px-NextSTEP_mars-soil-excavation.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1920" data-file-height="1080" /></a><figcaption>A NASA <a href="/wiki/ISRU" class="mw-redirect" title="ISRU">ISRU</a> system concept for autonomous robotic excavation and processing of Mars soil to extract water for use in exploration missions.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Several plans for a <a href="/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars" title="Human mission to Mars">human mission to Mars</a> have been proposed, but none have come to fruition. The NASA Authorization Act of 2017 directed NASA to study the feasibility of a crewed Mars mission in the early 2030s; the resulting report concluded that this would be unfeasible.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-290" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-290"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>288<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-291" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-291"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>289<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In addition, in 2021, China was planning to send a crewed Mars mission in 2033.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-292" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>290<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Private_spaceflight" title="Private spaceflight">Privately held companies</a> such as <a href="/wiki/SpaceX" title="SpaceX">SpaceX</a> have also <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_Mars_Colonization_Program" class="mw-redirect" title="SpaceX Mars Colonization Program">proposed plans to send humans to Mars</a>, with the eventual goal to <a href="/wiki/Colonization_of_Mars" title="Colonization of Mars">settle on the planet</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-293" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-293"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>291<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of 2024, SpaceX has proceeded with the development of the <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_Starship" title="SpaceX Starship">Starship</a> launch vehicle with the goal of Mars colonization. In plans shared with the company in April 2024, Elon Musk envisions the beginning of a Mars colony within the next twenty years. This would be enabled by the planned <a href="/wiki/Mass_manufacturing" class="mw-redirect" title="Mass manufacturing">mass manufacturing</a> of Starship and initially sustained by resupply from Earth, and <a href="/wiki/In_situ_resource_utilization" title="In situ resource utilization">in situ resource utilization</a> on Mars, until the Mars colony reaches full self sustainability.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-294" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>292<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Any future human mission to Mars will likely take place within <a href="/wiki/Exploration_of_Mars#Launch_windows" title="Exploration of Mars">the optimal Mars launch window</a>, which occurs every 26 months. The moon Phobos has been proposed as an anchor point for a <a href="/wiki/Space_elevator" title="Space elevator">space elevator</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-295" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-295"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>293<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Besides national space agencies and space companies, groups such as the <a href="/wiki/Mars_Society" title="Mars Society">Mars Society</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-296" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-296"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>294<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/The_Planetary_Society" title="The Planetary Society">The Planetary Society</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-297" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-297"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>295<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> advocate for human missions to Mars.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="In_culture">In culture</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Mars_in_culture" title="Mars in culture">Mars in culture</a> and <a href="/wiki/Mars_in_fiction" title="Mars in fiction">Mars in fiction</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Planets_in_astrology#Mars" title="Planets in astrology">Planets in astrology §&#160;Mars</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa,_original_graphic_15.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa%2C_original_graphic_15.jpg/250px-The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa%2C_original_graphic_15.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="314" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa%2C_original_graphic_15.jpg/500px-The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa%2C_original_graphic_15.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2392" data-file-height="3000" /></a><figcaption><i><a href="/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds" title="The War of the Worlds">The War of the Worlds</a></i> by <a href="/wiki/H._G._Wells" title="H. G. Wells">H. G. Wells</a>, 1897, depicts an <a href="/wiki/Alien_invasion" title="Alien invasion">invasion of Earth</a> by fictional Martians.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mars is named after the <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Rome" title="Ancient Rome">Roman</a> <a href="/wiki/Mars_(mythology)" title="Mars (mythology)">god of war</a> (<a href="/wiki/Greek_mythology" title="Greek mythology">Greek</a> <a href="/wiki/Ares" title="Ares">Ares</a>), but was also associated with the demi-god <a href="/wiki/Heracles" title="Heracles">Heracles</a> (Roman <a href="/wiki/Hercules" title="Hercules">Hercules</a>) by ancient Greek astronomers, as detailed by <a href="/wiki/Aristotle" title="Aristotle">Aristotle</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-DRGA&#95;Planetae&#95;298-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-DRGA_Planetae-298"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>296<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This association between Mars and war dates back at least to <a href="/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy" title="Babylonian astronomy">Babylonian astronomy</a>, in which the planet was named for the god <a href="/wiki/Nergal" title="Nergal">Nergal</a>, deity of war and destruction.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-299" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-299"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>297<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-300" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-300"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>298<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It persisted into modern times, as exemplified by <a href="/wiki/Gustav_Holst" title="Gustav Holst">Gustav Holst</a>'s orchestral suite <i><a href="/wiki/The_Planets" title="The Planets">The Planets</a></i>, whose famous first movement labels Mars "The Bringer of War".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-301" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-301"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>299<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Planet_symbols" class="mw-redirect" title="Planet symbols">The planet's symbol</a>, a circle with a spear pointing out to the upper right, is also used as a symbol for the male gender.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-302" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-302"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The symbol dates from at least the 11th century, though a possible predecessor has been found in the Greek <a href="/wiki/Oxyrhynchus_Papyri" title="Oxyrhynchus Papyri">Oxyrhynchus Papyri</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jones-1999&#95;303-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jones-1999-303"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>301<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><audio id="mwe_player_3" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" style="width:250px;" data-durationhint="456" data-mwtitle="Holst_-_The_Planets_-_Mars,_The_Bringer_of_War_(TMO_Orchestra,_Incheon,_2023).opus" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Holst_-_The_Planets_-_Mars,_The_Bringer_of_War_(TMO_Orchestra,_Incheon,_2023).opus"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/50/Holst_-_The_Planets_-_Mars%2C_The_Bringer_of_War_%28TMO_Orchestra%2C_Incheon%2C_2023%29.opus/Holst_-_The_Planets_-_Mars%2C_The_Bringer_of_War_%28TMO_Orchestra%2C_Incheon%2C_2023%29.opus.ogg" type="audio/ogg; codecs=&quot;vorbis&quot;" data-transcodekey="ogg" data-width="0" data-height="0" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/50/Holst_-_The_Planets_-_Mars%2C_The_Bringer_of_War_%28TMO_Orchestra%2C_Incheon%2C_2023%29.opus" type="audio/ogg; codecs=&quot;opus&quot;" data-width="0" data-height="0" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/50/Holst_-_The_Planets_-_Mars%2C_The_Bringer_of_War_%28TMO_Orchestra%2C_Incheon%2C_2023%29.opus/Holst_-_The_Planets_-_Mars%2C_The_Bringer_of_War_%28TMO_Orchestra%2C_Incheon%2C_2023%29.opus.mp3" type="audio/mpeg" data-transcodekey="mp3" data-width="0" data-height="0" /></audio></span><figcaption>The first movement of Gustav Holst's The Planets - Mars, The Bringer of War (TMO Orchestra, Incheon, 2023)</figcaption></figure>
<p>The idea that Mars was populated by intelligent <a href="/wiki/Martian" class="mw-redirect" title="Martian">Martians</a> became widespread in the late 19th century. <a href="/wiki/Giovanni_Schiaparelli" title="Giovanni Schiaparelli">Schiaparelli's</a> "canali" observations combined with <a href="/wiki/Percival_Lowell" title="Percival Lowell">Percival Lowell</a>'s books on the subject put forward the standard notion of a planet that was a drying, cooling, dying world with ancient civilizations constructing irrigation works.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-304" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-304"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>302<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many other observations and proclamations by notable personalities added to what has been termed "Mars Fever".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-fergus04&#95;305-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-fergus04-305"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>303<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the present day, high-resolution mapping of the surface of Mars has revealed no artifacts of habitation, but pseudoscientific speculation about intelligent life on Mars still continues. Reminiscent of the <i>canali</i> observations, these speculations are based on small scale features perceived in the spacecraft images, such as "pyramids" and the "<a href="/wiki/Cydonia_(Mars)" title="Cydonia (Mars)">Face on Mars</a>".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-306" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>304<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In his book <i><a href="/wiki/Cosmos_(Sagan_book)" title="Cosmos (Sagan book)">Cosmos</a></i>, planetary astronomer <a href="/wiki/Carl_Sagan" title="Carl Sagan">Carl Sagan</a> wrote: "Mars has become a kind of mythic arena onto which we have projected our Earthly hopes and fears."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sagan80&#95;246-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sagan80-246"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The depiction of Mars in fiction has been stimulated by its dramatic red color and by nineteenth-century scientific speculations that its surface conditions might support not just life but intelligent life.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lightman97&#95;307-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lightman97-307"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>305<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This gave way to many <a href="/wiki/Science_fiction" title="Science fiction">science fiction</a> stories involving these concepts, such as <a href="/wiki/H._G._Wells" title="H. G. Wells">H. G. Wells</a>'s <i><a href="/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds" title="The War of the Worlds">The War of the Worlds</a></i>, in which Martians seek to escape their dying planet by invading Earth; <a href="/wiki/Ray_Bradbury" title="Ray Bradbury">Ray Bradbury</a>'s <i><a href="/wiki/The_Martian_Chronicles" title="The Martian Chronicles">The Martian Chronicles</a></i>, in which human explorers accidentally destroy a Martian civilization; as well as <a href="/wiki/Edgar_Rice_Burroughs" title="Edgar Rice Burroughs">Edgar Rice Burroughs</a>'s series <i><a href="/wiki/Barsoom" title="Barsoom">Barsoom</a></i>, <a href="/wiki/C._S._Lewis" title="C. S. Lewis">C. S. Lewis</a>'s novel <i><a href="/wiki/Out_of_the_Silent_Planet" title="Out of the Silent Planet">Out of the Silent Planet</a></i> (1938),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sanford09&#95;308-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sanford09-308"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>306<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and a number of <a href="/wiki/Robert_A._Heinlein" title="Robert A. Heinlein">Robert A. Heinlein</a> stories before the mid-sixties.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-buker02&#95;309-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-buker02-309"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>307<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since then, depictions of Martians have also extended to animation. A comic figure of an intelligent Martian, <a href="/wiki/Marvin_the_Martian" title="Marvin the Martian">Marvin the Martian</a>, appeared in <i><a href="/wiki/Haredevil_Hare" title="Haredevil Hare">Haredevil Hare</a></i> (1948) as a character in the <a href="/wiki/Looney_Tunes" title="Looney Tunes">Looney Tunes</a> animated cartoons of <a href="/wiki/Warner_Brothers" class="mw-redirect" title="Warner Brothers">Warner Brothers</a>, and has continued as part of popular culture to the present.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-rabkin05&#95;310-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-rabkin05-310"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>308<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> After the <a href="/wiki/Mariner_program" title="Mariner program"><i>Mariner</i></a> and <a href="/wiki/Viking_program" title="Viking program"><i>Viking</i></a> spacecraft had returned pictures of Mars as a lifeless and canal-less world, these ideas about Mars were abandoned; for many science-fiction authors, the new discoveries initially seemed like a constraint, but eventually the post-<i>Viking</i> knowledge of Mars became itself a source of inspiration for works like <a href="/wiki/Kim_Stanley_Robinson" title="Kim Stanley Robinson">Kim Stanley Robinson</a>'s <a href="/wiki/Mars_trilogy" title="Mars trilogy"><i>Mars</i> trilogy</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-311" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-311"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>309<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1327269900" /><div>

</li>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="References">References</h2></div>
<div class="mw-references-wrap mw-references-columns"><ol class="references">
<li id="cite&#95;note-VSOP87-2"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-VSOP87_2-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><style <li id="cite&#95;note-science324&#95;5928&#95;736-67"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-science324_5928_736_67-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFMcSweenTaylorWyatt2009" class="citation journal cs1">McSween HY, Taylor GJ, Wyatt MB (May 2009). Archived from <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="http://daleandersen.seti.org/Dale_Andersen/Science_articles_files/Heldmann%20et%20al.2005.pdf">the original</a> <span class="cs1-format">(PDF)</span> on 1 October 2008<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">17 September</span> 2008</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=Journal+of+Geophysical+Research&amp;rft.atitle=Formation+of+Martian+gullies+by+the+action+of+liquid+water+flowing+under+current+Martian+environmental+conditions&amp;rft.volume=110&amp;rft.issue=E5&amp;rft.artnum=2004JE002261&amp;rft.pages=Eo5004&amp;rft.date=2005-05-07&amp;rft&#95;id=info%3Ahdl%2F2060%2F20050169988&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fapi.semanticscholar.org%2FCorpusID%3A1578727%23id-name%3DS2CID&amp;rft&#95;id=info%3Abibcode%2F2005JGRE..110.5004H&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fciteseerx.ist.psu.edu%2Fviewdoc%2Fsummary%3Fdoi%3D10.1.1.596.4087%23id-name%3DCiteSeerX&amp;rft&#95;id=info%3Adoi%2F10.1029%2F2004JE002261&amp;rft.aulast=Heldmann&amp;rft.aufirst=Jennifer+L.&amp;rft&#95;id=http%3A%2F%2Fdaleandersen.seti.org%2FDale&#95;Andersen%2FScience&#95;articles&#95;files%2FHeldmann%2520et%2520al.2005.pdf&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AMars" class="Z3988"></span> 'conditions such as now occur on Mars, outside of the temperature-pressure stability regime of liquid water'... 'Liquid water is typically stable at the lowest elevations and at low latitudes on the planet because the atmospheric pressure is greater than the <a href="/wiki/Vapor_pressure" title="Vapor pressure">vapor pressure</a> of water and surface temperatures in equatorial regions can reach 273 K for parts of the day [Haberle <i>et al.</i>., 2001]'</span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-nasa101001-177"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-nasa101001_177-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20100528175553/http://marsrover.nasa.gov/science/goal1-results.html">"Mars Exploration Rover Mission: Science"</a>. <li id="cite&#95;note-253"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-253">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://solarsystem.nasa.gov/missions/mariner-04/in-depth">"Mariner 04"</a>. <i>NASA Solar System Exploration</i>. 20 December 2017. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20200803192306/https://solarsystem.nasa.gov/missions/mariner-04/in-depth/">Archived</a> from the original on 3 August 2020<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">9 February</span> 2020</span>. <q>The Mariner 4 mission, the second of two Mars flyby attempts launched in 1964 by NASA, was one of the great early successes of the agency, and indeed the Space Age, returning the very first photos of another planet from deep space.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=NASA+Solar+System+Exploration&amp;rft.atitle=Mariner+04&amp;rft.date=2017-12-20&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fsolarsystem.nasa.gov%2Fmissions%2Fmariner-04%2Fin-depth&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AMars" class="Z3988"></span> <span class="noviewer" typeof="mw:File"><span><img alt="Public Domain" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/62/PD-icon.svg/20px-PD-icon.svg.png" decoding="async" width="12" height="12" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/62/PD-icon.svg/40px-PD-icon.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="196" data-file-height="196" /></span></span> This article incorporates text from this source, which is in the <a href="/wiki/Public_domain" title="Public domain">public domain</a>. <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/nmc/spacecraft/display.action?id=1964-077A">"Spacecraft – Details – Mariner 4"</a>. <i>NASA – NSSDCA</i>. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20180904174225/https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/nmc/spacecraftDisplay.do?id=1964-077A">Archived</a> from the original on 4 September 2018<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">9 February</span> 2020</span>. <q>Mariner 4...represented the first successful flyby of the planet Mars, returning the first pictures of the martian surface. These represented the first images of another planet ever returned from deep space.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=NASA+%E2%80%93+NSSDCA&amp;rft.atitle=Spacecraft+%E2%80%93+Details+%E2%80%93+Mariner+4&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fnssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov%2Fnmc%2Fspacecraft%2Fdisplay.action%3Fid%3D1964-077A&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AMars" class="Z3988"></span> <span class="noviewer" typeof="mw:File"><span><img alt="Public Domain" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/62/PD-icon.svg/20px-PD-icon.svg.png" decoding="async" width="12" height="12" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/62/PD-icon.svg/40px-PD-icon.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="196" data-file-height="196" /></span></span> This article incorporates text from this source, which is in the <a href="/wiki/Public_domain" title="Public domain">public domain</a>.</span>
</li>
